Background and Context

Overview Understanding the historical and cinematic context of Sunset Boulevard is essential to appreciating its form, themes, and tone. Billy Wilder’s 1950 film emerges from a period of immense transformation in Hollywood — from the decline of the silent era to the rise of the studio system and the eventual disillusionment of post-war America. The film reflects both the glamour and the decay of the entertainment industry, satirising its obsession with fame, morality, and illusion.

The Silent Film Era From the early 1910s until the late 1920s, cinema existed largely without synchronised sound. The silent era represented the height of early film artistry, when narrative was conveyed through title cards, exaggerated gestures, and musical accompaniment. Although these films demonstrated remarkable creativity, the arrival of sound — the so-called “talkies” — revolutionised the industry. Many silent stars found their careers abruptly curtailed as they struggled to adapt to dialogue-driven storytelling. This shift from visual expressiveness to vocal performance exposed the fragility of celebrity, a theme later echoed in Wilder’s portrayal of Norma Desmond. Her inability to transition from silent stardom to the spoken screen mirrors the real-life decline of many former icons.

Analysis The silent era symbolises a world of illusion sustained by artifice and image — a notion central to Sunset Boulevard . Wilder’s film draws nostalgia and melancholy from this lost cinematic world, exposing the cost of technological progress and the impermanence of fame.

The Golden Age of Hollywood From the 1930s through to the early 1960s, Hollywood entered what became known as its “Golden Age”. During this time, the major studios — MGM, Paramount, Warner Bros., and others — dominated the global film industry. By the mid-1940s, these studios produced hundreds of films annually, shaping popular imagination through idealised visions of love, success, and moral order. Films of this period were characterised by clear narratives, linear plots, and archetypal heroes and heroines. Their moral tone was often conservative, reinforcing social hierarchies and traditional gender roles. However, by the late 1950s, the system began to weaken under the pressures of television, shifting audiences, and artistic fatigue. However, the flim industry in the United States was going through a rough patch: Television was emerging as the dominant entertainment medium in the U.S. Weekly cinema attendance was declining. The Paramount anti-trust consent decree was implemented, requiring separate ownership for production companies and theatre chains. There was a population shift to the suburbs, impacting downtown movie theatres. There were a set of rules for moral guidelines that filmmakers had to follow under The Hays Code.

Analysis Wilder’s critique of Hollywood in Sunset Boulevard coincides with the decline of this era. The film’s depiction of faded glamour and moral corruption exposes the darker underbelly of an industry once devoted to producing dreams. Through satire, Wilder dismantles the myth of Hollywood’s innocence.

The Production Code Between 1930 and 1968, the film industry was governed by the Production Code Administration (PCA), a body that imposed strict moral guidelines on cinematic content. Initially voluntary, these regulations became mandatory by the mid-1930s and dictated that all films must uphold public decency and moral virtue. Depictions of nudity, profanity, excessive violence, or sexual behaviour were prohibited. Filmmakers were compelled to ensure that wrongdoing was punished and virtue rewarded. Wilder and his collaborator Charles Brackett often tested these boundaries, embedding subversive commentary within seemingly conventional narratives. In Sunset Boulevard , the tragic fates of Joe Gillis and Norma Desmond adhere to the code’s demand for moral consequence — the transgressors must pay for their sins. Yet Wilder’s irony turns this moralism on its head, inviting sympathy for both victim and perpetrator.

Analysis The Production Code’s influence gives the film a paradoxical tone — simultaneously moralistic and cynical. While the narrative satisfies censorship standards through punishment, it also exposes the hypocrisy of Hollywood’s moral pretensions. Wilder uses the very language of the Code to critique the system that enforced it.

Film Noir Sunset Boulevard belongs to the film noir tradition — a style originating in 1940s post-war America and named by French critics to describe the “dark films” emerging from Hollywood. Noir was marked by chiaroscuro lighting, urban settings, complex psychology, and an atmosphere of mistrust and fatalism. The genre’s visual style relied on unbalanced compositions, low-key lighting, and stark black-and-white contrasts, evoking a world of corruption and moral ambiguity. In tone, noir films explored alienation and moral decay, often narrated by disillusioned protagonists trapped by circumstance.

Analysis Wilder fuses noir aesthetics with Hollywood satire, using shadow and perspective to visualise moral collapse. Joe’s cynical narration, the claustrophobic mansion, and the interplay of light and darkness all reinforce themes of delusion, decay, and entrapment. The film thus becomes both a noir thriller and a self-reflexive critique of the industry that produced it.

America in the 1950s Post-war America entered a period of unparalleled prosperity and conservatism. The 1950s were shaped by the Cold War, the rise of consumer culture, and rigid gender expectations. Prosperity bred conformity, and political paranoia — intensified by McCarthyism and the House Un-American Activities Committee — fostered fear of dissent. Socially, women were expected to embody domestic virtue while men resumed positions of economic dominance. Yet beneath this façade of order lay widespread disillusionment and anxiety about identity and purpose. Sunset Boulevard channels these tensions through its portrayal of isolation, ambition, and moral corruption within an ostensibly glamorous world.

Analysis The film captures the contradictions of 1950s America: wealth and decay, progress and repression, fame and emptiness. Norma’s madness and Joe’s moral exhaustion personify the cultural malaise of a nation seduced by success yet haunted by its own discontents. Wilder’s critique of Hollywood thus becomes a mirror for post-war America’s soul.
Values in Sunset Boulevard
Social Values

Traditional Gender Roles The era upheld traditional gender roles, with men as breadwinners and women primarily responsible for homemaking and child-raising. The suburban housewife became an iconic image of the time. Norma’s character highlights the fading role of ageing actresses in Hollywood, mirroring the limited opportunities for women beyond traditional roles as wives and mothers.

Consumerism The 1950s were characterised by a post-war economic boom, and there was a strong emphasis on consumerism. Owning a home, car, and various consumer goods became aspirational goals. The opulent mansion on Sunset Boulevard and Norma’s extravagant lifestyle represent a distorted version of the consumerist dream, where wealth and possessions mask deeper issues.
Cultural Values

Hollywood’s Golden Age Hollywood’s Golden Age is a term used to describe a period in the history of the American film industry that roughly spans from the late 1920s to the early 1960s. It was a time of unprecedented creativity, innovation, and commercial success in Hollywood. The Studio System – a way of making movies where big companies, like Warner Bros. and Paramount, had total control over everything. They decided which actors to use, what movies to make, and even how the movies were shown in theatres.

Technological Advancements This era witnessed significant advancements in filmmaking technology, including the transition from silent films to ‘talkies’, the development of colour cinematography, and the use of widescreen formats.
The Star System The method of creating, promoting and exploiting stars in Hollywood films. Movie studios would select promising young actors and glamourise them through various promotion tactics.

Character Relationships

Joe Gillis and Norma Desmond Joe and Norma’s relationship is built on dependence, illusion, and emotional imbalance. Norma clings to Joe as a last connection to youth and relevance, while Joe allows himself to be financially supported, though emotionally trapped. Their dynamic is parasitic and destructive, culminating in Joe’s death and Norma’s complete psychological collapse.

Key Quotes Joe: ’Norma, you’d be throwing it away. I don’t qualify for the job, not any more.’ Norma: ’Is it funny that I’m in love with you?’ Joe: ’Wake up, Norma. You’d be killing yourself to an empty house. The audience left twenty years ago.’ Norma: ’You’re not leaving me!’
Key Ideas Dependency Reality vs. Illusion Power imbalance Gender roles

Joe Gillis and Betty Schaefer Joe and Betty’s relationship symbolises hope, creativity, and authentic emotional connection. While Joe is initially cynical, Betty helps him reconnect with his talent and ideals. However, Joe’s entrapment in Norma’s world ultimately sabotages their bond.

Key Quotes Betty: ’If you love me, Joe.’ Joe: ’Three cheers for Betty Schaefer! I will now kiss that nose of yours.’ Betty: ’Joe, I can’t look at you any more.’ Joe: ’Good luck to you, Betty... When you and Artie get back... here’s the pool.’
Key Ideas Authenticity Missed opportunities Love vs. obligation Hope and disappointment

t: Norma Desmond and Max Von Mayerling
b: Norma Desmond and Max Von Mayerling Max and Norma share a hauntingly devoted and co-dependent relationship. Once her director and husband, Max now lives as her butler, fuelling her illusions to protect her fragile psyche. His loyalty borders on tragic obsession.

Key Quotes Max: ’I made her a star. I cannot let her be destroyed.’ Max: ’It was I who asked to come back... I was her first husband.’ Max: ’The cameras have arrived, Madame.’ Norma: ’All right, Mr. deMille, I’m ready for my close-up.’
Key Ideas Loyalty Delusion Power reversal Sacrifice

t: Norma Desmond and Cecil B. deMille
b: Norma Desmond and Cecil B. deMille Norma views deMille as the key to her return to fame, though he treats her with nostalgia and pity. Their relationship highlights the **Hollywood power shift** from silent films to sound, and the industry’s neglect of ageing stars.

Key Quotes Norma: ’Now Mr. deMille can wait till I’m good and ready.’ deMille: ’Thirty million fans have given her the brush. Isn’t that enough?’ Norma: ’We’ll be working again, won’t we, Chief?’ deMille: ’Tell him to forget about our car. I’ll buy him five old cars if necessary.’
Key Ideas Fame and decay Nostalgia vs. reality Hollywood’s cruelty Gendered ageism

t: Joe Gillis and Max Von Mayerling
b: Joe Gillis and Max Von Mayerling Though they barely trust one another, Joe and Max are both caught in Norma’s gravitational pull. Max warns Joe of Norma’s delusions but ultimately enables them, silently observing Joe’s downfall.

Key Quotes Max: ’You must understand, I discovered her when she was eighteen.’ Max: ’Madame is the greatest star of them all... I will take Mr. Gillis’ bags.’ Joe: ’Oh, that. It’s from a friend of mine. A middle-aged lady, very foolish and very generous.’
Key Ideas Powerlessness Enabling behaviour Masculinity Pity and rivalry

Joe Gillis and Artie Green Joe and Artie are casual friends, connected through Betty. Artie is warm, generous, and unsuspecting—offering Joe a place to stay and unknowingly introducing him to Betty, who will fall in love with Joe. Their relationship represents Hollywood’s working-class camaraderie, but it is ultimately undermined by Joe’s betrayal.

Key Quotes Artie: ’Hey Joe, I said you could have my couch, I didn’t say you could have my girl.’ Artie: ’Where you been keeping that gorgeous face of yours?’ Artie: ’Well what do you know, Joe Gillis.’
Key Ideas Friendship Jealousy and betrayal Trust

Betty Schaefer and Artie Green Betty and Artie are engaged, but her growing admiration for Joe shifts the dynamic. Artie symbolises comfort and stability, while Joe represents ambition and excitement. Their relationship quietly dissolves as Betty’s emotional attention shifts.
Key Quotes Artie: ’Wait a minute, this is the woman I love, who was loaded.’ Betty: ’Of course I love him. I always will. I’m just not in love with him any more.’
Key Ideas Love vs. stability Unspoken heartbreak Emotional conflict

Joe Gillis and Sheldrake Sheldrake represents the industry gatekeepers—pragmatic and dismissive. Joe’s interactions with him underscore how impersonal and transactional Hollywood has become. Their relationship exposes the disillusionment of struggling writers.

Key Quotes Sheldrake: ’Alright Gillis, you’ve got five minutes, what’s your story about?’ Joe: ’You’d have turned down Gone With the Wind .’ Sheldrake: ’Put in a few numbers, that’d make a cute musical.’
Key Ideas Hollywood as business Artistic compromise Creative frustration

Norma Desmond and Betty Schaefer Although they never interact directly, Norma and Betty represent **opposing ideals of womanhood**. Betty is young, modern, and practical; Norma is older, delusional, and clinging to a past era. Their symbolic rivalry culminates in Norma’s jealousy-fuelled murder of Joe.
Key Quotes (Implied Tension) Norma (to Joe): ’Is it a woman? I know it’s a woman...’ Joe: ’All right, baby. This way out.’ Betty: ’If you love me, Joe.’
Key Ideas Rivalry Gendered insecurity Age and beauty

t: Norma Desmond and Cecil B. deMille
b: Norma Desmond and Cecil B. deMille Norma idolises deMille as her old mentor, believing he will revive her career. deMille pities Norma, indulges her briefly, but ultimately distances himself. Their relationship exemplifies Hollywood’s betrayal of its past stars.

Key Quotes Norma: ’Now Mr. deMille can wait till I’m good and ready.’ deMille: ’Thirty million fans have given her the brush. Isn’t that enough?’ Norma: ’We’ll be working again, won’t we, Chief?’ deMille: ’A dozen press agents working overtime can do terrible things to the human spirit.’
Key Ideas Nostalgia Fame and decline Compassion vs. cruelty

t: Max Von Mayerling and Betty Schaefer
b: Max Von Mayerling and Betty Schaefer Though they never meet, Max and Betty represent two opposite relationships with Joe: one built on silent loyalty and loss of self (Max), the other on mutual respect and potential for growth (Betty). Max is the ghost of what Joe could become if he fully submits to Norma.
Key Quotes (Implied Contrast) Max: ’I directed all her early pictures. I made her a star.’ Betty: ’I want to write. I don’t want to be a reader all my life.’
Key Ideas Self-sacrifice vs. ambition Emotional entrapment vs. liberation Generational contrast

Sheldrake and Betty Schaefer Betty and Sheldrake represent opposite ends of the studio power structure. Sheldrake controls opportunities and trivialises creative work, while Betty strives to have a voice as a writer. Their professional relationship reflects the dismissiveness of studio executives toward aspiring creatives.
Key Quotes Sheldrake: ’Make it a girls’ softball team... it’d make a cute musical.’ Betty: ’I kind of hoped to get in on this deal. I don’t want to be a reader all my life.’

Key Ideas Industry hierarchy Gender and creative voice Power and dismissiveness subsection Joe Gillis and Cecil B. deMille Joe’s view of deMille is shaped by disillusionment; deMille represents the **industry’s indifference** toward people like Norma and himself. Their interaction is limited, but deMille’s treatment of Norma is indirectly relayed to Joe—highlighting Hollywood’s tendency to sentimentalise the past while refusing to truly engage with it.
Key Quotes deMille: ’Thirty million fans have given her the brush. Isn’t that enough?’ Joe (on deMille): ’He didn’t have the heart to tell you. None of us has had the heart.’
Key Ideas Fame and abandonment Disillusionment with power Truth vs. kindness

Betty Schaefer and Norma Desmond Though Betty and Norma never speak directly, their relationship exists as a powerful symbolic contrast—two women representing vastly different eras, values, and roles in Hollywood. Betty is young, ambitious, and grounded in reality; Norma is ageing, delusional, and obsessed with regaining lost fame. They become rivals not only for Joe’s affection but also as emblematic opposites: one looking forward, the other trapped in the past. Norma’s jealousy toward Betty culminates in Joe’s death—making their indirect relationship one of the most consequential in the film.

Key Quotes Betty: ’It’s not your career — it’s mine. I kind of hoped to get in on this deal. I don’t want to be a reader all my life. I want to write.’ Norma: ’Is it a woman? I know it’s a woman...’ Norma: ’You’re not leaving me!’ Joe (to Betty): ’Good luck to you, Betty... here’s the pool.’
Key Ideas Generational conflict Female ambition vs. faded fame Jealousy and romantic rivalry Hope vs. delusion

Relationship with Ambition Ambition is a desire and determination to achieve success. It is a motivational force that compels individuals to set and pursue goals, often with a willingness to work hard, overcome obstacles, and make sacrifices to attain those goals.

Norma Desmond Norma’s ambition is rooted in her desire to regain her past Hollywood stardom and in her fear of becoming irrelevant. It is also sustained by her delusional belief in her continued desirability as an actress, despite Hollywood moving on. Norma’s isolation and the presence of enablers who validate her fantasies further fuel her ambition and detachment from reality. In response to the word comeback: ‘I hate that word. It’s a return, a return to the millions of people who have never forgiven me for deserting the screen.’

Joe Gillis Joe’s ambition arises from financial desperation and a desire for Hollywood success. His pursuit of financial security and career advancement drives him into a complex relationship with Norma. ‘The poor dope - he always wanted a pool. Well, in the end, he got himself a pool.’

Betty Schaefer Betty is driven by her genuine passion for screenwriting and a strong desire to succeed in the film industry. ‘What’s wrong with being on the other side of the cameras? It’s really more fun.’

Max von Mayerling Max’s ambition is solely devoted to serving and preserving Norma Desmond’s illusion of fame. He is driven by his unwavering dedication to Norma, having been her director and husband during her stardom. ‘Madame is the greatest star of them all.’
Relationship with Delusion

Joe’s agent, Morino Wilder characterises Joe’s agent Morino as a representative of the cutthroat nature of Hollywood. Morino says he could give Gillis money… but he won’t. Instead, Gillis should use his desperate state as inspiration – it’ll force him to write because he’ll need to do it.

Betty Schaefer Betty is portrayed as a down-to-earth, sincere, and practical character. She represents a departure from the delusional world of Hollywood, focusing on her genuine passion for screenwriting and her desire for authentic, meaningful connections.

Norma Desmond ‘All right, Mr. DeMille, I’m ready for my close-up.’ In the film’s iconic closing line, Norma’s delusional state of mind reaches its peak, marking her descent into madness. While she retained some connection to reality in earlier scenes, this quote signifies the complete erasure of any remaining self-awareness. Norma regresses to her past glory, even mistaking Max for director Cecil DeMille, vividly highlighting the profound transformation of her fantasy world.

Joe Gillis ‘You don’t yell at a sleepwalker – he may fall and break his neck. That’s it: She was still sleepwalking along the giddy heights of a lost career.’ Joe compares Norma to a sleepwalker, suggesting she is unaware of her actions and the reality of her situation, emphasising the delicate balance between preserving her illusions and confronting the truth of her faded career.

Max von Mayerling ‘That is my job. It has been for a long time. You must understand I discovered her when she was eighteen. I made her a star. I cannot let her be destroyed.’ By nourishing her illusions and crafting fan mail, Max sees himself as Norma’s guardian, dedicated to preserving her faded fame. Yet, as he fervently conceals the truth to protect her from despair, his deceptions inadvertently propel her further into madness.

Film Techniques

List of Techniques Film Techniques and Metalanguage Film technique / metalanguage term Definition / Effect Close-up shot Tightly frames a person or a subject. Enables the audience to see the emotions of a character or details on a subject. Extreme close-up A more intense close-up that might focus on a specific feature. Mid shot Usually shows an actor from the waist up. Focus is on the characters but also shows the environment they are in. Long shot Used to show a subject from a distance, establishing them within a setting. Establishing shot Usually the first shot of a scene to establish the location and environment. Panning shot When the camera moves horizontally from its fixed position and mirrors the motion of looking from side to side. Point of view shot Mimics what a character in a scene is seeing, putting the audience into their perspective. Framing The composition of characters or subjects within a single shot. Two shot A medium shot showing two actors in a single frame. Mise en scène All elements contained in a single frame (lighting, costuming, positioning, set, props). Low angle shot Subject is shot from below eye level to make them appear powerful, dominant, or morally superior. High angle shot Subject is filmed above eye level to make them appear vulnerable or weak. Aerial shot A shot with the camera positioned directly above a subject. Over the shoulder shot A medium close-up shot from behind another character’s shoulder, typically used in conversations. Diegetic

Analysis of Particular Scenes Sunset Boulevard is perhaps the most famous film about film. A darkly funny yet disturbing noir, it follows washed-up screenwriter Joe Gillis being pulled into the murky world of even-more-washed-up former silent film star Norma Desmond, disingenuously helping with her screenplay. Critical commentary on the film industry is obviously included here, but Billy Wilder’s 1950 film digs deeper to explore the blurred line between fantasy and reality, as well as power, authenticity and self-delusion. Crucially, these themes are often shown in the film’s construction, via the cinematic techniques implemented by Wilder in each scene. This subsection will explore the most important examples of these cinematic techniques. Remember, VCE examiners are on the lookout for students who can offer a close reading of the text they are discussing, giving specific examples of how its creator has constructed it to support their arguments. Just look at the difference between an essay that says: ’Through the final shot of the film, Wilder shows Norma completely succumbing to her fantasy.’ Compared to one that argues: ’Through his utilisation of an increasingly glossy and distorted filter in the ominous final shot, Wilder depicts Norma being completely overtaken by her romanticised fantasy of ‘Old Hollywood’.’

t: Camera Techniques: Shot Types and Angles
b: Camera Techniques: Shot Types and Angles Camera techniques are arguably the primary way that a director will intentionally direct the eye of the audience, directly framing how they view a film. The two most basic ways in which the camera is used for this are through the distance between the subject (what the scene is about) and the camera, or the ‘shot type’ and the ‘camera angle’ at which the subject is being filmed. First Appearance of Norma Desmond Our first look at Norma Desmond is within the wide shot above, just as Joe Gillis has entered her dishevelled mansion early in the film. As a rule, the introductory shot of a character is always worth closely analysing, as the director typically establishes their characteristics and place within the film’s wider world. Shown above, this distant first look at Norma establishes her distance, both physical and mental, from the world around her. Removing herself from an industry that has long since moved on from her, she is severely out of touch with the reality of the world outside her home. Crucially, as this same shot is from Joe’s perspective, Wilder also foreshadows the more specific character ‘distance’ that will emerge between the two. Here, the audience sees the space Joe will similarly leave between himself and Norma, disingenuously humouring her poor-quality scripts and romantic advances and, therefore, always keeping her ‘at a distance’. The Final Goodbye Another shot conveying crucial information about character relation

Key Examples of Camera Angles Opening Title Just like the introductory shot of a character is worth digging into, the opening shot of a film is also incredibly important to unpack. Sunset Boulevard’s seemingly straightforward opening shot simply includes the film’s title, by showing the real-life Hollywood street. However, notice that we are not seeing a ‘Sunset Boulevard’ street sign (the more obvious choice), but instead a dirty and stained curbside. Further, Wilder shoots this curb from a high angle. Therefore, the film’s opening shot establishes maybe the most central aim of Wilder’s film; offering a critical look at the superficiality and flawed nature of Hollywood. As such, we are literally looking down on the film industry in the first moment of the film, represented by this dirty and unflattering visual symbol of Hollywood. This, therefore, is setting the stage for the satire and critical commentary that will follow. After Losing Betty Wilder’s careful use of camera angles is further shown at the end of the film after Betty abandons Joe at the gate of Norma’s mansion. Crucially, this all happened due to the desperate exertion of power by Norma, who called Betty and revealed the details of her relationship with Joe. As such, Wilder shoots Norma at a low angle, as Joe looks up at her haughty gaze. The level of power that Norma has exerted over Joe may seem minimal within the moment, but when we consider what happens next, this shot becomes much more important. On the br

Mise-en-Scène Mise-en-scène is perhaps the most deceptively simple cinematic technique. It involves analysing what appears within a frame and where it has been placed by the director. This includes elements such as the actor’s costumes, the props and the design of the set. Often, mise-en-scène is used to reinforce something we are being told about a character already through the film’s dialogue and acting. Joe’s Apartment I Joe’s Apartment II We can see a key example of characterisation through mise-en-scène early in the film, where the audience’s introduction to Joe Gillis visually communicates his unconcerned and detached attitude, as well as his tendency to settle for something convenient despite its inauthenticity. His being dressed in a bathrobe with the blazing sun outside (and his debt collectors clearly up and doing their jobs) speaks to his slovenliness and uninvested approach to life. The set design within this scene further characterises Joe, with the script directly describing the ‘reproductions of characterless paintings’ that cover his walls. Here, the set arguably provides a visual metaphor for the profit-driven ‘Bases Loaded’ script he is writing at that very moment, later described by Betty as having come ‘from hunger.’ Norma’s Bedroom Norma’s Living Room Equally, our introduction to the home of Norma Desmond helps establish the key elements of her character. The house is, as Joe describes, ‘crowded with Norma Desmonds’, in the form of countless framed photos

Symbols in Scenes Chimp One of the more seemingly inexplicable parts of Wilder’s film actually contains one of its most important symbols, with Norma’s pet monkey playing a key foreshadowing role from beyond the grave. The chimp, a pet owned and trained by Norma to amuse her, leaves a vacant role that Joe will gradually fill after having unknowingly interrupted its funeral. From this point in the film, Joe is manipulated, or ‘trained’, by Norma to entertain and provide companionship to her. Naturally, Joe also ends up dead within the bounds of Norma’s estate, with this symbol, therefore, foreshadowing the full trajectory of his character. All of this is directly alluded to through Joe’s description of the ‘mixed-up dream’ he has the night of the funeral, imagining ‘an organ [player]’ and the ‘chimp…dancing for pennies’ that he will soon become. Organ This naturally brings us to the organ itself, which serves as a physical reminder of the unflattering parts of the new role Joe must play. Included after Joe wakes from his ‘mixed-up dream’, the shot above frames Max’s organ-playing hands as massive and overpowering, as the much-smaller Joe storms in demanding to know why his ‘clothes and things’ were moved to Norma’s house without his say-so. Crucially, Norma then reveals that she ordered this action and that Joe’s apartment debts are ‘all taken care of’, hand-waving his attempt at grasping back some control and dignity by proposing it be ‘deduct[ed]...from [his] salary’. This s

Allusions Finally, we come to allusions, one of the techniques that Sunset Boulevard is most famous for. Allusions refer to anytime something from outside the world of the text is referenced, including other texts and real-world people, places, events, etc. Biblical and mythological allusions are commonly found in fiction, but references to something closer to our world can often bring a degree of realism to certain texts, working to strengthen their social commentary.

Symbols The Mansion Symbolises Norma’s decaying fame and psychological isolation A crumbling structure, ’out of beat with the rest of the world’ Reflects her detachment from reality and the passage of time Black-and-White Cinematography Enhances contrast between Norma’s world (dark, stagnant) and Betty’s world (bright, full of life) Reinforces themes of nostalgia and decay Spotlights Symbolise attention, former fame, and Norma’s obsession with being seen Used dramatically when Norma sits in deMille’s chair – a ghost-like figure in the light Gates and Barriers Represent isolation, exclusion, and entrapment Norma uses her home as a prison to keep herself—and Joe—separated from the real world The Monkey’s Funeral Symbolises Norma’s lost career and her inability to let go of the past Gothic mis-en-scène highlights the absurdity and tragedy of her delusion Cars Represent control, freedom, and social mobility Joe’s loss of his car mirrors his loss of independence when he becomes trapped in Norma’s world Close-Ups of Norma’s Face Reflect her silent film origins and the over-expressiveness required in that era Symbolise her inability to adapt and her clinging to outdated forms of identity and performance The Salome Script Symbolises Norma’s desperate attempt to reclaim her stardom and control her narrative Reflects her delusion and obsession with youth, power, and seduction A vehicle through which she imagines her triumphant return, though the industry has no real interest in it Para

Introduction and Context

Author Background Jane Harrison, a descendant of the Muruwari people, is an acclaimed playwright, novelist, and critic. As a child, she “always loved books” but was disheartened by the absence of Aboriginal representation in Australian literature. In writing Rainbow’s End , Harrison sought to give voice to those experiences that had long been silenced or marginalised. Drawing upon oral histories from Aboriginal Elders, she captures the resilience and humour of families who lived on “The Flats” and later at Rumbalara—the First Nations housing estate in Mooroopna. Harrison’s work strongly condemns the substandard housing and systemic neglect endured by First Nations families during the 1950s. Through her writing, she positions herself as a conduit for truth-telling, giving dramatic expression to both the hardships and triumphs of Aboriginal life. Her aim, as she has stated, is to use fiction as “an important way for First Nations people to have a voice.”

Analysis Harrison’s authorial perspective intertwines personal heritage and political conviction. By dramatizing the everyday realities of Aboriginal families, she reclaims history from the margins and rehumanises voices often reduced to statistics or stereotypes. Her compassionate yet incisive storytelling challenges audiences to confront both the past and its lingering presence in modern Australia.

Historical Context The colonisation of Australia, beginning with the arrival of the First Fleet in 1788, unleashed devastating consequences for First Nations peoples. The ensuing Frontier Wars—violent conflicts between Aboriginal people and British settlers—resulted in the massacre and displacement of countless Indigenous communities. Throughout much of the twentieth century, government policy continued to pursue assimilation and cultural eradication. One of the most tragic outcomes of these policies was the forced removal of Aboriginal children from their families. These children, later known as the Stolen Generations, were stripped of their language, names, and cultural identity. Many were placed in institutions or adopted into white families, where they were exploited as unpaid labour and suffered emotional and physical abuse. In Rainbow’s End , Harrison threads this history through the lives of Nan and Gladys, whose trauma reflects the generational wounds inflicted by colonial intervention. Gladys’s experience of being taken from her family and made to work as a domestic servant mirrors the broader policy of forced assimilation. Her obsession with Dolly’s education becomes a personal act of restitution—an attempt to reclaim what was taken from her by systemic injustice.

Analysis By embedding the historical trauma of dispossession within intimate family narratives, Harrison compels her audience to recognise the enduring emotional and social consequences of colonisation. Her portrayal of the Stolen Generations not only condemns past wrongs but also foregrounds the resilience and agency of those who survived them.

The Flats and Cummeragunja Cummeragunja , founded in 1888 on Yorta Yorta/Bangerang Country, represented both refuge and resistance for Aboriginal people. Initially a self-sufficient farming community, it was later undermined by the New South Wales Protection Board, which seized control in 1915. Aboriginal workers were paid in meagre rations, and funds generated by their labour were appropriated by the Board. By the 1930s, disease and poverty had decimated the community. In 1939, the residents of Cummeragunja staged a historic protest—the first mass strike by Aboriginal Australians. Many left the mission and settled on “The Flats,” a floodplain near Mooroopna and Shepparton. Despite harsh living conditions, including humpies made from “flattened kero tins” and exposure to extreme weather, the community retained its cultural vitality and kinship ties. By the early 1950s, around 300 people lived there.

Analysis The Flats serve as both a literal and symbolic setting in Rainbow’s End . They embody dispossession yet also community, poverty yet perseverance. Through this setting, Harrison contrasts the instability imposed by colonial structures with the cultural rootedness of Aboriginal families. The Flats become a testament to the capacity of a people to adapt, resist, and endure despite systemic neglect.

t: Analysis of the Historical and Cultural Context in Rainbow’s End
b: Analysis of the Historical and Cultural Context in Rainbow’s End

Overview Jane Harrison’s Rainbow’s End emerges from a landscape shaped by colonisation, segregation, and resilience. Set in 1950s Australia, the play reflects both the harsh realities and quiet triumphs of Aboriginal families living under systemic discrimination. The historical context is indispensable to understanding the characters’ identities, the injustices they face, and the endurance that defines their humanity. Harrison’s work gives voice to the unsung heroes—especially women—who sustained their communities despite persistent marginalisation.

Acknowledgement and Purpose The play’s thematic foundation begins with an acknowledgment of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples as the traditional custodians of the land. This recognition situates Rainbow’s End within an ethical framework that honours history and memory. Harrison’s intent is not merely to tell a story but to preserve a cultural legacy and challenge erasure. Commissioned to highlight Aboriginal women’s experiences, the play transforms domestic life into political narrative, demonstrating that resistance often begins within the home.

Analysis By embedding acknowledgment into the narrative fabric, Harrison bridges past and present. The act of remembering becomes both art and activism, inviting audiences to reflect on how storytelling can restore dignity to those history overlooked. Her focus on Aboriginal women redefines heroism as endurance and nurturing in the face of dispossession.

Historical Background: Colonisation and Segregation The origins of the play’s conflict trace back to the legacy of colonisation and government policies such as the Protection Acts, which sanctioned segregation and the forced removal of children. Aboriginal families were confined to reserves like Kamarra Ganja, where strict management and limited access to resources entrenched poverty. These lived realities form the backbone of Harrison’s narrative world, revealing how state control sought to diminish autonomy while families fought to preserve it.

Analysis Harrison transforms historical record into lived drama. The reserve system becomes a stage for both oppression and resilience. By drawing from documented injustices, she crafts a narrative that demands empathy and accountability. The audience confronts how legislative cruelty translated into generational trauma.

t: Life on the Reserves and the Flats
b: Life on the Reserves and the Flats After the walk-off from Kamarra Ganja, many Aboriginal families relocated to makeshift settlements known as “the flats.” These spaces, though impoverished, became hubs of culture, laughter, and communal strength. The families’ persistence in maintaining cultural traditions symbolises defiance against systemic neglect. In this environment, children learned resilience as a form of inheritance, and hope became both a necessity and an act of protest.

Analysis The depiction of life on the flats captures a paradox — material deprivation coexists with emotional and cultural richness. Harrison celebrates community cohesion as a radical response to marginalisation. The domestic setting transforms into a microcosm of resistance where identity is preserved through humour, storytelling, and maternal leadership.

t: Government Policy and the Metaphor of the Rainbow
b: Government Policy and the Metaphor of the Rainbow The government housing schemes of the 1950s, such as those in Rumbalara, promised integration but perpetuated segregation through inadequate living conditions. The metaphor of the rainbow recurs as a powerful symbol throughout the play. To the characters, the “end of the rainbow” represents dreams of equality and acceptance — visions that remain just out of reach. This unattainable horizon mirrors the historical struggle for recognition endured by Aboriginal Australians.

Analysis The rainbow encapsulates the duality of hope and despair. It is both a dreamscape and a mirage — a reflection of the distance between aspiration and reality. Harrison employs this image to expose the cruel optimism embedded within promises of assimilation. The characters’ pursuit of the rainbow becomes a metaphor for the broader national pursuit of reconciliation still incomplete.

The 1950s Social Landscape Post-war Australia was marked by conservative values, economic expansion, and racial inequality. For Aboriginal peoples, this period intensified the contradictions between visibility and exclusion. Women, in particular, bore the burden of navigating these intersecting oppressions. Through Gladys, Dolly, and Nan Dear, Harrison portrays how Aboriginal women negotiated identity, motherhood, and dignity within a society determined to silence them.

Analysis Harrison’s representation of 1950s Aboriginal womanhood dismantles stereotypes of passivity. Her characters inhabit a world of limited choices yet wield moral and emotional power. Their courage lies not in grand rebellion but in persistence — in cleaning, teaching, loving, and speaking truths that white Australia refused to hear.

Conclusion Understanding the historical and cultural context of Rainbow’s End enriches every reading of the play. Harrison’s depiction of segregation, displacement, and resilience transforms history into human experience. The struggles of the Banks and Dear families are not isolated but emblematic of a broader narrative of survival. The play stands as both testimony and challenge: to remember, to acknowledge, and to act. In recognising the truths of the past, Harrison invites audiences to see the enduring strength of Aboriginal identity — not as tragedy, but as triumph.

Structure of the Play Rainbow’s End opens in medias res , plunging the audience directly into the aftermath of a flood. This sudden beginning creates an atmosphere of crisis and displacement, immediately reflecting the instability that defines the characters’ world. The play’s structure is unconventional—beginning with a prologue, followed by scenes divided into parts A and B, reflecting fragmentation and lack of control in the characters’ lives. Harrison integrates dream sequences that blur the boundary between fantasy and reality. These imaginative interludes, such as Gladys’s visions of meeting the Queen or Dolly’s aspirations for education, express both hope and futility. The juxtaposition of dreams and deprivation highlights the gap between aspiration and circumstance. Two narrative threads underpin the play: Gladys’s political awakening and Dolly and Errol’s romance. Each culminates in an epiphany—Gladys’s courageous confrontation with the Shire Council and Dolly’s recognition of Errol’s unconscious racial assumptions. The play concludes with two significant symbols: Nan’s dream of Dolly’s wedding and the song “Que Sera, Sera.” The latter, appearing at both the beginning and end, transforms from a cynical reflection of government indifference into a subtle anthem of hope and change. Harrison’s structural choices mirror her thematic concerns. The fragmented narrative echoes the disrupted lives of Aboriginal families, while the interweaving of dreams and realism underscor

t: Structure and Themes in Rainbow’s End
b: Structure and Themes in Rainbow’s End

Overview Jane Harrison’s Rainbow’s End is constructed as a two-act play divided into short, interlinked scenes that illuminate the resilience, struggles, and dreams of First Nations families in 1950s Australia. The fragmented structure mirrors the fractured social and political realities of Aboriginal life, where moments of humour and hope coexist with hardship and prejudice. Harrison’s manipulation of stage direction, song, and dialogue builds dramatic tension and emotional intimacy, guiding the audience through a tapestry of generational experience.

Act Structure and Thematic Design Unlike traditional plays divided by chapters, Rainbow’s End unfolds through acts and scenes that offer glimpses into domestic and emotional moments. This episodic format reflects the uncertainty that governs the Dear and Banks families’ lives. Scenes often end abruptly or transition during moments of crisis, symbolising the instability caused by poverty and discrimination. The two major narrative threads — Gladys’s political awakening and Dolly’s coming-of-age romance — develop in parallel, converging at the climax where both women redefine their strength and identity.

Analysis Harrison’s two-act structure underscores her social commentary. The brevity and rhythm of scenes intensify audience empathy while exposing the invisible pressures imposed by systemic inequality. By structuring the play around fragmented lives, Harrison creates a dramatic form that mirrors cultural and emotional dislocation — yet also reveals endurance and connection within the Aboriginal matriarchal family.

t: In Media Res and Dramatic Openings
b: In Media Res and Dramatic Openings The play begins in media res amid the chaos of a flood, a moment both literal and symbolic. Accompanied by the recurring song “Que Sera, Sera,” the opening sequence immediately juxtaposes hope with helplessness. This song returns at the play’s conclusion, binding the narrative through a motif that evokes the tension between aspiration and acceptance.

Analysis Beginning in the middle of action thrusts the audience into the characters’ reality without exposition, reflecting the immediacy of their struggle for survival. The flood motif encapsulates emotional and social inundation, while the musical refrain serves as both comfort and irony — a lullaby masking pain.

Dream Sequences and Juxtaposition Dream sequences punctuate the play, revealing the characters’ inner lives and unfulfilled desires. Gladys’s fantasy of meeting the Queen becomes a recurring image of colonial aspiration — an impossible vision shaped by internalised subservience and social exclusion. These dreams contrast sharply with the family’s reality in a riverside shack, where dignity must be defended daily.

Analysis Through dream imagery, Harrison critiques the illusion of social mobility within a racist society. The juxtaposition of fantasy and deprivation exposes the psychological toll of colonisation, while simultaneously affirming the imagination as a site of survival and self-worth.

Character Growth and Narrative Resolution Both major storylines reach their culmination in acts of self-realisation. Dolly challenges her mother’s fixation on external validation, forcing Gladys to confront the limits of her own deference. This confrontation propels Gladys toward activism, culminating in her powerful declaration at the Shire meeting: “We demand proper schooling … The white people too — they need to be educated about us” (p.197). Though the play ends with personal growth, systemic barriers remain unresolved, reflecting the persistence of structural inequality.

Analysis The dual transformation of Gladys and Dolly demonstrates Harrison’s belief in intergenerational empowerment. Their evolution symbolises a shift from private endurance to public assertion — a necessary transition for community progress. The incomplete resolution emphasises realism over idealism, reinforcing that the struggle for equality continues beyond the stage.

Dramatic Intensity and Unseen Forces Harrison’s use of short, tightly constructed scenes sustains dramatic tension and emphasises the relentlessness of external pressures. Many of the most significant events occur offstage, their impact conveyed through dialogue and reaction. The small cast heightens intimacy while unseen antagonistic figures — landlords, officials, and employers — personify the faceless nature of institutional discrimination.

Analysis By keeping oppression unseen yet omnipresent, Harrison amplifies its psychological weight. The absence of visible villains shifts focus toward resilience, love, and humour as tools of survival. The minimal staging and interwoven dialogue allow audiences to inhabit the emotional pulse of the characters’ world, where silence often speaks louder than confrontation.

Conclusion The play’s structure functions as both narrative device and political metaphor. Through acts, scenes, dreams, and songs, Harrison crafts a world where fragmented experiences reveal collective truth. The rhythm of the play mirrors the cycles of struggle and renewal within Aboriginal communities, inviting readers and audiences to engage with the ongoing dialogue between past injustices and present resilience. Understanding the form of Rainbow’s End thus deepens appreciation for its portrayal of identity, endurance, and hope in mid-twentieth-century Australia.

t: Audience and Purpose in Rainbow’s End
b: Audience and Purpose in Rainbow’s End

Overview Jane Harrison’s Rainbow’s End (2005) was written to educate and engage both Indigenous and non-Indigenous audiences through its portrayal of three generations of Aboriginal women — Nan Dear, Gladys, and Dolly — living in 1950s Victoria. Harrison’s purpose extends beyond recounting hardship; she celebrates resilience, dignity, and familial love amidst oppressive social structures. The play invites audiences to confront the consequences of colonisation and assimilation while recognising the strength of community and cultural continuity.

Purpose of the Play Harrison’s central purpose is to foreground the untold stories of Aboriginal women and their everyday heroism. Through the intimate domestic setting of the Dear and Banks family, she challenges the invisibility imposed upon First Nations peoples by historical narratives. Her play functions as both cultural preservation and political statement — illustrating that Indigenous survival is not defined by victimhood but by persistence and pride. Harrison’s writing embodies the principle that art can both memorialise and mobilise. By humanising historical trauma, she transforms policy and prejudice into personal experience, allowing audiences to empathise rather than merely observe. The play’s tone oscillates between warmth and sorrow, comedy and critique, mirroring the emotional complexity of survival.

Analysis Through Rainbow’s End , Harrison asserts that storytelling is a mode of reclamation. Each scene restores voices historically silenced by government policy and social discrimination. Her purpose is not only to depict resilience but to invite truth-telling — to reposition Aboriginal women as custodians of memory, agency, and identity.

Intended Audience Rainbow’s End addresses a dual audience. For non-Indigenous viewers, it acts as an awakening — revealing the lived experiences obscured by whitewashed Australian history. For Indigenous audiences, it affirms continuity and pride, validating intergenerational strength and the endurance of culture. By positioning humour and tenderness alongside hardship, Harrison ensures accessibility for classroom and theatre audiences alike. The play encourages educational engagement, prompting critical reflection among students while offering Aboriginal spectators the comfort of recognition. The inclusion of intergenerational female perspectives fosters empathy across age groups and cultural divides.

Analysis Harrison’s nuanced approach ensures that the play operates on multiple emotional and intellectual levels. Non-Indigenous audiences are confronted with complicity in structural injustice, while Indigenous audiences are offered representation that transcends trauma. This balance of challenge and affirmation defines the play’s enduring impact within Australian theatre and education.

Historical Awareness and Cultural Responsibility The play’s authenticity stems from Harrison’s meticulous research and personal connection to her Murrawari heritage. Drawing upon oral histories, newspaper archives, and community interviews, she constructs a historically grounded yet emotionally resonant narrative. Her inclusion of real figures, such as Pastor Doug Nichols, situates the fictional world within an authentic cultural framework. This fidelity to truth underscores Harrison’s responsibility as both playwright and cultural custodian. Her depiction of the 1950s assimilation era reflects an ethical commitment to remembrance — to ensuring that audiences understand the policies that fractured families and communities, and the resilience that rebuilt them.

Analysis Harrison’s creative purpose merges documentation with dramatisation. She transforms history into a living dialogue between past and present, ensuring that Aboriginal experience is neither mythologised nor forgotten. By embedding historical authenticity within intimate storytelling, she teaches audiences that understanding cultural identity begins with acknowledging historical reality.

t: The Role of Representation in Theatre
b: The Role of Representation in Theatre For Harrison, representation is activism. The play reclaims space within the Australian canon for Aboriginal voices, particularly those of women. Through humour, vernacular speech, and emotional candour, she dismantles stereotypes and restores humanity to communities too often reduced to statistics. Rainbow’s End thereby becomes a vessel for truth-telling, reconciliation, and education.

Analysis Representation in Rainbow’s End is not symbolic but lived — an act of cultural assertion. Harrison’s purpose is fulfilled when audiences see Aboriginal women not as passive subjects of history but as authors of their own narratives. The play’s enduring presence in school curricula ensures that these voices continue to educate, inspire, and transform generations of Australians.

Conclusion Harrison’s Rainbow’s End unites purpose and audience in a dialogue of empathy, education, and recognition. Her portrayal of three Aboriginal women transcends their fictional identities to stand as symbols of cultural endurance. By crafting a story grounded in truth and delivered with compassion, Harrison reshapes the Australian stage into a site of reconciliation — where understanding begins through listening, and hope endures through storytelling.
Language in Rainbow’s End

Overview Language in Jane Harrison’s Rainbow’s End is a powerful vehicle for expressing identity, class, and cultural conflict. The characters’ speech patterns reveal their personal histories and social positions, while Harrison’s authorial voice is embedded within their dialogue, reflecting her own commentary on racial inequality, resilience, and generational change. Language, therefore, functions not only as a mode of communication but also as an indicator of power, prejudice, and pride within the 1950s Australian context.

Nan Dear Nan’s language is colloquial, humorous, and often sharply ironic. When she informs Gladys that she “won’t be needing any of them encyclops” (p.136), she dismisses education’s relevance to her family’s daily struggles, revealing both her pragmatic worldview and the limited opportunities afforded to her generation. Her unpolished speech reflects her lower-class status and lack of formal education—an outcome of systemic exclusion faced by Aboriginal Australians. Yet Nan’s language also injects comedy into the play. Her dry humour and literal phrasing create moments of levity that highlight resilience in hardship. When mocking Gladys’s excitement about meeting the Queen, Nan’s irony underscores the absurdity of colonial class structures. Through Nan’s speech, Harrison reveals both the pain and pride of a woman grounded in survival, whose humour becomes an act of quiet rebellion against social inequality.

Analysis Nan’s dialect situates her within a marginalised social class while asserting authenticity and self-respect. Her vernacular speech becomes an emblem of identity, opposing assimilationist pressures that sought to erase Aboriginal voice. Harrison uses Nan’s language to celebrate oral tradition and defiant humour, challenging notions that education alone defines intelligence.

Dolly Banks Dolly’s language reflects her education and confidence but also her awareness of the social hierarchies constraining her. She is direct and assertive, particularly when confronting racism. Her rebuke of Errol’s remark about “you people” (p.141) exposes his unconscious bias and the normalisation of racial division. Likewise, her challenge to her mother — “You’re always telling me to stick up for myself, but when do you, eh?” (p.162) — forces Gladys to examine her internalised inferiority. Dolly also code-switches, adopting Indigenous vernacular when she threatens to “kick Ester’s boys up to moon” (p.137), using humour and familiarity to assert belonging within her community. Her fluency, both in educated English and colloquial Aboriginal dialect, embodies her dual identity as a bridge between two worlds. Her closing assertion to Errol — that she would never trade her family or heritage for a “better life” (p.172) — epitomises pride and defiance.

Analysis Dolly’s language signifies empowerment through articulation. Harrison crafts her speech to demonstrate that mastery of language is not assimilation but assertion. Dolly’s ability to speak for herself—and against injustice—makes her a vehicle for Harrison’s advocacy of Indigenous self-determination. Her linguistic versatility becomes symbolic of cultural strength rather than concession.

Errol Fisher Errol’s halting and awkward speech reflects both his nervousness and his ignorance. As an encyclopedia salesman who depends on persuasion, his incompetence is ironically comic. When he awkwardly notes that Dolly’s glass is “made out of a can” (p.140), he unwittingly insults her. His language often reveals class and racial privilege cloaked in politeness — exemplified in his use of “you people” (p.140) to generalise Aboriginal Australians. His verbal missteps and tone-deaf expressions highlight his sheltered upbringing and lack of cultural awareness. Over time, however, Errol’s language softens and becomes more considerate, mirroring his emotional growth and respect for Dolly’s world.

Analysis Harrison uses Errol’s speech to critique the well-meaning but ignorant paternalism of mid-century white Australia. His progression from patronising to empathetic reflects the broader social journey toward understanding and equality. His linguistic awkwardness thus serves as a tool of both comedy and commentary.

Gladys Banks Gladys’s voice develops from timid dreamer to assertive activist. During the Rodney Shire meeting, her passionate declaration — “We demand proper schooling … The white people too — they need to be educated about us, and our ways” (p.197) — becomes a turning point where her speech echoes Harrison’s own political message. Her earlier language is often infused with fantasy and aspiration: daydreams of the Queen’s attention or Dolly’s graduation soften her struggles. Yet by the play’s end, Gladys has found a political and moral voice that embodies the collective frustrations of Indigenous Australians denied dignity. Her transition from domestic to public speech represents empowerment through language.

Analysis Through Gladys, Harrison demonstrates the transformative power of speech as activism. The act of “speaking up” replaces passive dreaming with agency. Gladys’s voice becomes that of her people — articulate, righteous, and unafraid — asserting the necessity for visibility and reform.

The Royals The Queen’s language is formal, elevated, and exclusionary. Her reference to Australia as “the birthplace of the nation” (p.124) erases 65,000 years of Aboriginal civilisation, while her statement about standing “at last among Australians” implicitly refers only to white citizens. This polished rhetoric embodies colonial arrogance. By contrast, the Duke’s language is more spontaneous and unguarded. His offhand question — “Where’s Father?” (p.127) — humanises him but also underscores the royal couple’s detachment from the lived realities of those they visit.

Analysis Harrison employs the Royals’ diction as social critique. Their words — ceremonious, detached, and self-congratulatory — expose the gulf between imperial symbolism and the Indigenous suffering it conceals. The Queen’s non-inclusive language mirrors the nation’s non-inclusive identity at mid-century.

The Radio Announcer The radio announcer’s language is superficially cheerful yet profoundly condescending. In describing the royal visit, he mentions a demonstration of “how to throw a boomerang” and marvels that it “really does come back” (p.128). His tone trivialises a complex cultural artefact, reducing Indigenous skill to entertainment for the royal couple.

Analysis The announcer’s commentary encapsulates institutional racism disguised as enthusiasm. Harrison’s use of irony highlights how mainstream media perpetuated ignorance, framing Aboriginal culture as novelty rather than heritage. His words parody the way national pride excluded First Nations people.

Structural and Symbolic Language Beyond dialogue, Harrison uses language in her stage directions and symbolism to reinforce meaning. Descriptions of the Dear family’s humpy — “waterlogged” and “mud-spattered” (pp.123, 176) — visually communicate poverty and marginalisation. The title itself, Rainbow’s End , draws on the myth of a pot of gold, suggesting the illusion of prosperity and the unattainability of a promised better life. The reference to “Somewhere Over the Rainbow” from The Wizard of Oz introduces an ironic hopefulness. Its lyrics — “the dreams that you dare to dream really do come true” — echo the family’s optimism but also question the realism of those dreams in a society unwilling to grant equality.

Analysis Harrison’s manipulation of both literal and figurative language constructs a layered critique of Australia’s racial landscape. The interplay between hope and futility in her metaphors underscores the central tension of the play — between aspiration and limitation, imagination and reality. Language, whether spoken, written, or symbolic, thus becomes both a weapon and a wound.

Love and Belonging Harrison explores love and belonging in rainbows end, with Nan and Gladys creating a warm, loving home for Dolly. When Errol offers Dolly a “better life” in the city with a “real home,” Dolly is taken aback by his naivety to her culture. Even though the humpy had its downside, Dolly acknowledged that it’s the fierce love and loyalty that made living there easier. Nan. Sometimes, you have to move on. Leave things behind. Even things you love. (P.187) Dolly. No matter how they come into the word, you still love’em the same. (P.186) Errol. What matters is [her] / not [her] address (p. 149). Nan. Oh, for heaven’s sake, Dolly marry that boy, before someone else does. (P.198)

Leadership Leadership is explored through Gladys’ speech where she took Papa Dears place at the petition. Harrison displays not only the leadership in the family dynamic but also the government taking leadership over the indigenous Australians and believing that they know what is best for them and displaying leadership in a very different way. This is displayed by the Inspector as being the character showing the government in ‘Rainbow’s End’. Inspector: “but someone must take leadership” Gladys: “I will present the petition” Gladys: “She needs to know the world is bigger than just this.” (pg 131) Gladys: “I’m not an interloper- I belong here –this is my land!” Gladys: “We’ve been humble for too long.” Gladys: “I make the decisions regarding Dolores, thank you.” Nan Dear: “Grab the flour, Glad, and the tea.” Gladys: “Oi, Re the so-called ‘housing problem’, it is only a problem because of us Aboriginals-” Nan Dear: “I don’t care what you think! You and your visiting hours. Your rules. No singalongs after dark. Your spying. You, mister, can go to blazes! I’ll give you ‘one’...” Gladys: “We demand to be heard.”

t: The Intersection of Class and Race
b: The Intersection of Class and Race - The intersection of race and class in Rainbow’s End is exemplified by the dynamic between the white population and the Indigenous population. This relationship is explored in scenarios such as Dolly’s search for job, in which it is seen as unlikely she will be able to receive employment. This creates the inescapable cycle that is forged when race meets class, that to escape a lower class, it’s required to achieve a job that could be considered higher status to society. Race hinders this progress due to the prejudices and barriers held against Indigenous people. - The inspector’s view in regard to the flood, as he seems to have no awareness of the effect this will take upon first nations families, particularly the Dear family. - Gladys having to save every penny to afford the encyclopedias just to invest in the cultural capital. - Profit from proximity to whiteness (town Aboriginals) - Government policies Inspector: “property can be replaced” “I’d kind of like to be a nurse”- Dolly, which she believes to be silly.
