t: Connection to Land and the Importance of Home
b: Connection to Land and the Importance of Home - When the floods hit nan, dolly and Gladys, there house is destroyed and they are forced to move out of their home , the importance of home comes from Gladys when she is determined to “ Fix my own house.” although Gladys does not have the money to fix her home she is sharing the important of the connection to the land by not leaving and letting the white population win. Dolly also expresses the importance of home when Errol and dolly argue about leaving the flats to live in the city, dolly tells errol that her world is about family rather then leaving them for what errol calls a real home in the city. “I’m gunna go back and die there”- pg. 125 nan Gladys: “I’m not an interloper – I belong here – this is my land! “a real home is where there are people looking out for each other”- pg. 172 dolly Nan Dear: “Least here we do things our way.” The Rumbalara housing is “white and featureless” and “Anything but loveable.” (Stage directions) Radio: “the most vigorous attempt yet to solve Aboriginal housing” Gladys: ‘[I’m going] to fix my own house.” Errol: “It’s like your family’s from another country or something” Dolly: “Our life isn’t perfect, but like Nan says, it’s ours.” Gladys: “People is what matters.” Gladys: “Mrs Windsor, would you live at Rumbalara? Then why is it good enough for us?”

t: Types of Knowledge and Ways of Knowing
b: Types of Knowledge and Ways of Knowing Much of the family’s energy is expended on searching for access to the institutions of the white majority. Gladys never had time for learning, to busy to earn a living to have time to make up for the education that she was denied when she was removed from her home. Gladys has knowledge that far eclipses that of the ‘encyclops boy’. Harrison addresses the sparce of the colonial model of education and the aboriginal knowledge and ways of learning have faced since colonisation.
Character Analysis

Dolly Banks Dolly is the teenage daughter of Gladys and granddaughter of Nan Dear. Throughout the play, she serves as a receptacle for her mother’s dreams and her grandmother’s fears. Gladys fantasies often about what a better life might look like, but she doesn’t believe that such a life is possible for her. Instead, she dreams on Dolly’s behalf, and she tries to encourage Dolly to pursue goals that seem impossible, like getting a job at a bank that does not want to hire Aboriginal employees. Nan Dear, meanwhile, views Dolly as vulnerable to the same discrimination and violence that Nan Dear and Gladys have experienced. She is especially wary when Dolly begins seeing Errol, a White boy. Dolly’s relationship with Errol is complicated by Errol’s unconscious biases, and she temporarily breaks things off with him when he is unable to put aside his assumption that White middle-class life is superior to the more community-based life Dolly wants. Her relationship with Errol also enrages Dolly’s cousin Leon, who sexually assaults her and leaves her pregnant. Despite the violent origin of the pregnancy, Dolly loves her baby and names it after Papa Dear, cementing baby Regina’s status as a beloved member of the family included in the traditions and legacy it carries.

Analysis Dolly Banks is a crucial character in Jane Harrison’s play "Rainbow’s End," capturing the experiences of a young Aboriginal woman during the 1950s, a time rife with changes and challenges for her community. The daughter of Gladys Banks and the granddaughter of Nan Dear, Dolly is a character who embodies the bridge between the prior generation’s traditional views and the new generation’s aspirations. Throughout the play, Dolly asserts her desires and individuality in a society that would typically dismiss them. In one scene, she interacts with Errol, a character symbolising the broader society’s views, displaying her self-assured yet personable character. When Errol calls her "Miss Banks," she promptly corrects him, saying, "Dolly. We’re not too fussed about fancy titles," indicating both her lack of pretense. Errol complements her name, but once he learns her full name is Dolores, Dolly momentarily moves away, reflecting a subtle consciousness of boundaries and self-respect as she navigates her interactions with a non-Aboriginal man. Dolly’s aspirations and accomplishments are a point of pride for her mother, Gladys, who notes that Dolly is the first in the family to complete her Leaving Certificate, a testament to her intelligence and drive. The circumstances in which this achievement is presented, however, bring to light the stark realities of racial prejudices they confront. Gladys holds a photo of Dolly, which prompts the bank manager to acknowledge Dolly’s physi

Gladys Banks Gladys is Nan Dear’s middle-aged daughter and Dolly’s mother. As a child, Gladys was taken away from her family by the government and put to work as a maid for a White family. As such, she never learned to read, and the only way she could make a life for herself was to follow in her mother’s footsteps as a manual labourer. Despite the hardship she has faced, Gladys is relentlessly optimistic. She frequently enters into fantasy sequences where the world is just, her family is happy, and Dolly has endless possibility before her. Even outside those fantasy sequences, Gladys believes that Dolly can live a better life than the generations of women before her. She is steadfast in this belief, even when Dolly herself doubts it. Gladys’s optimism proves a vital tool for her eventual activism. After Dolly accuses Gladys of projecting her own dreams onto Dolly, Gladys commits herself to working toward making the world a better place for the sake of herself and her family. She confronts the city council on the radio about Aboriginal housing, and she takes over Papa Dear’s speech at a public presentation of a petition when he fails to arrive on time. Gladys’s optimism lets her imagine and believe in a better future, which in turn empowers her to fight for that future.

Analysis Gladys Banks represents the aspirations and challenges of an Aboriginal woman in 1950s Australia. This multi-dimensional character is caught between her cultural heritage and the desire for progress and recognition within the broader, often unfriendly, society. As the daughter of Nan Dear and mother to Dolly, Gladys mediates between the old ways and new possibilities that her daughter may have. A scene in the play that captures her ambitions and protective instincts as a mother is her confrontation with the bank manager where she presents her daughter Dolly for a teller’s position. Proudly asserting "She’s just completed her Leaving Certificate—the first in the family—with real good grades… ’N’ top of her class in algebra," Gladys is met not with recognition but indifference, with the bank manager pouring himself tea but pointedly ignoring her. The scene captures the societal barriers that Gladys and her daughter face, as well as Gladys’s determination to push against them. Gladys’s agency is further highlighted in her firm stance on her daughter’s autonomy and social life. Despite Nan Dear’s apprehensions, Gladys declares decisively, "I’ll make the decisions regarding Dolores thank you. She’s going to the ball. And Errol Fisher is walking her home." This reveals her resolve and the assertion of her role as a parent, as well as the modernising viewpoint she holds compared to Nan Dear, who is more rooted in traditionalist concerns. Moreover, Gladys’s readiness to conf

Nan Dear Nan Dear is the matriarch of the family at the centre of the play. She is Gladys’s mother and Dolly’s grandmother, and she takes her duty of care to them seriously. Nan Dear is a bastion of tradition, teaching Dolly about her family history and aspects of their culture that she doesn’t learn at school. She has suffered under institutions of racism and ideologies of White supremacy, especially at the Aboriginal reserve in Cummeragunja, where Nan Dear was abused and her daughter Gladys was taken away from her. As such, Nan Dear has little faith that the world can improve, and she doesn’t understand Gladys and Dolly’s attempts to improve it. She especially fears for Dolly when Dolly begins a romantic relationship with Errol, a White boy with the same last name as a White man who raped Nan Dear years ago. Nan Dear works to separate Dolly and Errol, but at the end of the play she realises that Errol is a considerate and respectful young man. When she finds out for certain that Errol is not related to her attacker, she gives him her blessing and encourages Dolly to marry him, demonstrating that even someone as traditional as Nan Dear can grow and change.

Analysis Born by the Murray River as her birth certificate states, Nan Dear is steeped in her Aboriginal heritage and connections to her traditional lands. Her identity is closely tied to the Murray River and the land from which she was displaced , specifically from Cummeragunja, marking a bitter and forcible removal from her home. This dislocation is a source of deep-seated resentment and shapes her worldview. Despite the trials she has faced, Nan Dear is characterised by a deep sense of duty and pragmatism . She is concerned with everyday survival and the practical needs of her family—working to put food on the table and keeping her family safe. As the matriarch, Nan Dear’s relationships with her daughter,Gladys, and granddaughter, Dolly, are a mix of affection and stern guidance. She is quick to offer wisdom to Dolly, such as reminding her of the importance of knowing family connections to avoid unwitting relationships with close relatives. Nan Dear’s knowledge of family history is implied to be extensive, though she may feign ignorance when it suits her, suggesting a complicated relationship with the past. Her cultural beliefs surface subtly through her reluctance to embrace the visiting monarchy, mocking the idea of needing white gloves for a royal visit and declaring, "She’s not my queen". This resistance to the monarch’s visit and the detachment she expresses towards Gladys’ interest in royalty reveals an underlying rejection of colonial authority and ap reference for

Errol Fisher Errol Fisher is a young White encyclopedia salesman. He encounters Nan Dear, Gladys, and Dolly by accident, after taking a wrong turn on his route selling encyclopedias. Errol falls in love with Dolly and comes to admire her family. Gladys demonstrates a strength that Errol has never seen in his own mother, who is subservient to Errol’s father. The affection that Nan Dear and Gladys show Dolly is also alien to Errol, who has grown up with a strict and unfeeling father. Though he earnestly loves Dolly, he offends her with his ignorance of her culture and his assumption that his way of life must be superior to hers. He assumes she would be eager to leave her family behind to be his wife in the city, and he struggles to understand her insistence that she must remain with her loved ones. Ultimately, though, Errol comes to respect what Dolly wants for her future, and he promises to live any kind of life she wants as long as they can do so together.

Analysis Fisher Errol Fisher emerges in Jane Harrison’s "Rainbow’s End" as a significant, though less central, character, who embodies the intersection of the Aboriginal community’s life and the broader Australian society during the 1950s. Portrayed initially as somewhat awkward and uncertain, he is introduced to the audience as he nearly falls off his bike upon encountering Dolly, signaling an immediate spark of attraction that complicates his interactions with the family. This initial interaction with Dolly sets the stage for Errol’s character development. The exchange with Gladys further illustrates his social position and perspective. When he tentatively approaches Gladys as she is chopping wood, mistaking her for a man, he immediately demonstrates cultural ignorance and nervousness. Apologising and stating his name, "Sorry er ma’am. My name is Errol Fisher," he provides a contrast to the women’s more assertive presence. This contrast is emblematic of the interplay between white Australians and Aboriginal people at the time—a dynamic of presumed authority and misunderstanding from the former, contrasted with an under-recognised competence and resilience from the latter. Errol’s intentions are primarily associated with business—selling encyclopedias as revealed in his conversation with Gladys—yet this transactional engagement serves as a backdrop to more complex intercultural exchanges. Gladys and Errol mention the encyclopedias simultaneously, indicating their previous in

Leon (Cousin) Leon, often referred to as simply “Cousin,” is Dolly’s cousin. He is aggressive and often drunk, and he disapproves of Dolly’s relationship with Errol. As a twisted form of punishment for that relationship, Leon rapes Dolly, resulting in her pregnancy. Leon’s assault of Dolly speaks to the reality of gendered violence within marginalised communities.

Papa Dear Papa Dear is Nan Dear’s husband, Gladys’s father, and Dolly’s grandfather. Though he is not Gladys’s biological father, since Gladys was conceived from Nan Dear’s rape by a White man, Nan Dear considers him Gladys’s father. Papa Dear is an activist for Aboriginal rights, and his work takes him all over the country, which means he is rarely there for his family. He never appears in person in the play; he is only mentioned in dialogue and imagined in fantasy sequences. His absence leaves Nan Dear, Gladys, and Dolly alone to create an exclusively feminine space in their home.

Analysis Dear Papa Dear, as depicted in Jane Harrison’s "Rainbow’s End," is a paternal figure whose presence is felt throughout the narrative despite being physically absent for much of the play. His character is crafted through the recollections and expectations of the other characters, particularly Nan Dear and Gladys. Papa Dear’s role is largely communicated through anecdotes and his influence on the family’s structure and values. Mentioned by Nan Dear as "busy doing good work. God’s work and hard work," Papa Dear is portrayed as a figure devoted to the betterment of the Aboriginal community. His dedication to activism and community work is further highlighted when Gladys recalls that he is "in Western Australia. Touring the communities there," and has been featured "in the newspaper and all," suggesting his actions and efforts are recognised and appreciated beyond their immediate surroundings. Papa Dear’s dream visitations reinforce his endearing place within the family, despite his absence. In an imagined scene, he is seen wearing "an old-fashioned hat and coat," hinting at a sense of nostalgia and timelessness associated with him. His brief entrance to kiss Gladys on the top of the head showcases a symbolic and loving connection with his family, despite his lack of direct intervention in their current struggles. The character of Papa Dear is particularly important to Dolly during a vulnerable moment when she reflects on her family tree, considering naming her unborn chi

The Inspector The Inspector is a White man employed by the Australian government to inspect the living conditions of Aboriginal Australians, which includes the family’s humpy. As a representative of the Aboriginal Protection Board, the Inspector regulates and controls the lives of Aboriginal Australians, and that power extends to taking Aboriginal children away from their family. The Board took Gladys away from her family when she was a child, and she and Nan Dear are terrified that the Inspector will take Dolly away from them.

Analysis In the play "Rainbow’s End," the inspector is a minor but telling character that presents a snapshot of the authority and surveillance that Aboriginal people endured in 1950s Australia. He appears in a scene where Nan and Gladys are inside the humpy, anxiously straightening everything, which sets a tone of tension and unease. The inspector serves as a symbol of the government oversight and control that loomed over the lives of the characters. Paying a visit to evaluate their living conditions, the inspector enters with a superficially pleasant demeanor that thinly veils the oppressive power dynamics at play. He greets the efforts of Nan and Gladys with an offhand compliment, "I say crocheted pillow shams. Such beautiful work!" which Gladys attributes to her mother’s handiwork. His interaction is polite yet distant, making notes while seemingly ignoring the weight of his presence and authority in the humpy. As the scene unfolds, it becomes clear that his visit is not a casual or friendly one, but an inspection that could have severe implications for the family. The anxiety Nan and Gladys display is indicative of their vulnerability to the whims of the inspector and, by extension, the colonial power structure. When the inspector inquires after a Mr. Banks, his question underscores the traditional views of family structure and the expectation of a male head of household, hinting at patriarchal values as well. The inspector’s questions and his mode of taking notes encaps

Mr. Coody Mr. Coody is the rent collector at Rumbalara. He imposes strict and invasive rules on his tenants, which includes threatening to evict Dolly and her family due to Dolly’s pregnancy. His intrusion into the women’s lives reflects that although Rumbalara is an Aboriginal housing district, it is not a safe haven or a stable place of community because it is run by White men.

Bank Manager The Bank Manager works at the bank where Gladys wants Dolly to find a job. He resists the idea of hiring an Aboriginal worker, making excuses as to why Dolly would not be qualified for the job, but Gladys finally stands up to him and he offers Dolly an opportunity to apply.

Analysis Harrison uses the The Bank Manager to showcase the systemic barriers faced by the Aboriginal community in terms of employment and societal inclusion. His interaction with Gladys Banks in her attempt to secure a job for her daughter, Dolly, is telling of the racially prejudiced attitudes of the time. Gladys, with determination, engages the Bank Manager and puts forward Dolly’s qualifications for a teller’s position: "But she’s just completed her Leaving Certificate—the first in the family." Despite the qualifications presented, the Bank Manager dismisses the notion, indicating a lack of openness to hiring an Aboriginal person. The Bank Manager focuses on Dolly’s appearance rather than her capabilities when he says, "Yes. Very pretty face," after examining a photograph of her. This superficial assessment underscores the racial objectification and segregation of Aboriginal people. His concern regarding how Dolly would "fit in" at the bank delineates the invisible boundaries placed by a society reluctant to accept Aboriginal individuals into professional roles. "In a job such as this reliability is important," he states, questioning how Dolly would commute to work, despite evidence of her punctuality and capability presented by Gladys. The role of the Bank Manager, while not overly complex, provides a window into both the institutionalised racism of the period and the socioeconomic status of Aboriginal families. His patronising attitude when offering a tin money bank to

Ester Ester is a neighbour of the family who represents the most vulnerable members of the Aboriginal community. She is regularly abused by her White husband, and her sons are taken away by the Inspector.
Regina Regina is Dolly’s infant daughter. Her birth marks the beginning of the fourth generation of women in the family.
Character and Relationships

Dolly Banks Key Quote: “And as far as what you’re offering … no thank you. This is my place. I’m staying right here with my mum and my nan.” (to Errol, p.172) Dolly’s non-reaction to her ruined magazines indicates a practised stoicism in the face of misfortune. She is studious, completing her Leaving Certificate with all ‘As and Bs’ and coming ‘top of her class in algebra’ (p.163). Remarkably ‘pretty’, she is also ‘clever’, ‘kind’, ‘modest’, and ‘straightforward’ (p.190). Dolly sometimes allows herself to dream, like Gladys winning Pick-A-Box (p.130) or winning the Miss Mooroopna-Shepparton title at the ball (p.166). She dreams fancifully of becoming an actress or model, yet practically of a career in nursing (p.142). She is unhappily aware that she might become a fruit picker like her mother and grandmother (p.141). Dolly’s exposure to racial discrimination — with ‘stones’ thrown at her and ‘snide remarks’ (p.162) overheard in the street — makes her pessimistic about her future. She has a close and loving relationship with Nan, whose overprotectiveness can sometimes be tedious. Yet their closeness and trust alleviate Dolly’s distress after she becomes pregnant, when Nan confides that Gladys was conceived in similarly traumatic circumstances. The sharing of this dark ‘secret’ brings them both comfort.

Analysis Dolly Banks embodies the tensions between youthful hope and social limitation. Harrison portrays her as the bridge between aspiration and resignation — a young woman educated enough to imagine a different future but aware of the barriers imposed by race and class. Her determination to remain with her family, rather than abandon them for the city life Errol proposes, symbolises her rejection of assimilation and her embrace of identity. Dolly’s relationship with Nan and Gladys represents the generational continuum of Aboriginal womanhood: endurance, wisdom, and resistance to erasure.

Nan Dear Key Quote: “Ask him what it says in that there encyclops about the Aboriginies, eh?” (to Gladys, p.135) Nan Dear is often the cynical voice of experience, reminding Gladys that she is a lowly bean picker unworthy of the Queen’s notice (p.124). Unafraid to speak out, she angrily confronts the Rent Collector for criticising their supposed ‘flouting’ of rules, throwing eggs at him (pp.185–6). Capable and energetic, she works as a fruit picker for the canning factory, tackles physical labour, and can make a stew out of nettles when money is short. She is a loving and protective grandmother who knows the danger of being ‘[taken] advantage of’ by ‘a whitefella’ (p.187). Set in her ways, she mistrusts outsiders — seeing Bob Dyer as ‘one of them flash Yanks’ (p.131) and regarding Errol as a ‘snake-oil salesman’ (p.136). Initially doubtful, she later recognises Errol’s goodness and advises Dolly to marry him ‘before someone else does’ (p.198). Nan also clashes with Gladys over Dolly’s upbringing, criticising her monarchist sympathies and sceptical of education’s value. Yet when she hears Gladys speak up on the radio, she dances with pride around the room.

Analysis Nan Dear represents endurance, tradition, and the persistence of Aboriginal matriarchal strength. Her scepticism toward authority and colonial structures masks deep compassion and wisdom. Through her character, Harrison celebrates the resilience of older generations who, despite trauma and displacement, preserve dignity and family unity. Nan’s humour, practicality, and protectiveness make her both a guardian of the past and an emblem of cultural survival.

Gladys Banks Key Quote: “We demand proper schooling. And not just for us. The white people too – they need to be educated about us, and our ways.” (p.197) Initially a fervent royalist, Gladys goes to extraordinary lengths to glimpse the Queen, only to be blocked by hessian screens hiding Indigenous homes from sight (p.127). Clever and capable, she answers all questions correctly on the quiz show Pick-A-Box but knows a ‘black’ contestant would be unwelcome (pp.129–130). She manages her household with efficiency and intelligence — chopping wood, skinning rabbits (p.129), and managing coins in jam tins — making the Bank Manager’s condescension (p.164) absurd. Having been sent away as a child (p.184) and denied an education, Gladys feels the sting of illiteracy and wants her daughter to succeed where she could not. Her daydreams of Dolly in academic regalia or being hugged by the Queen (pp.126, 134) express both hope and escapism. When Dolly challenges her to ‘fix’ her own life (p.179), Gladys redirects her energy toward activism — storming a council meeting and demanding fair treatment for Aboriginal families (p.181).

Analysis Gladys is a figure of transformation — from dreamer to activist. Her evolution from naive royalist to outspoken advocate mirrors the awakening of Indigenous political consciousness. She bridges the domestic and political spheres, proving that even an ordinary woman’s voice can challenge entrenched injustice. Harrison uses her courage to illustrate the theme of self-determination and the belief that change begins within the individual.

Errol Fisher Key Quote: “This is my first presentation. Well, the first one I got all the way through, anyhow.” (p.137) Errol is awkward, well-meaning, and naïve — a White encyclopedia salesman who stumbles into the Flats by mistake. His clumsy sales pitch and fearful reaction to a harmless carpet snake (p.145) reveal both humour and ignorance. Despite his flaws, his sincerity sets him apart from other male characters. His relationship with Dolly follows the familiar rhythm of romance fiction — a high at the ball, when he rescues her from Leon (p.167), and a low when he offends her by suggesting she abandon her family (p.171). Misunderstandings later see him wrongly blamed for Dolly’s assault, yet his determination to return and embrace her world reflects genuine love.

Analysis Errol’s character illustrates the possibility of cross-cultural understanding, albeit imperfect and halting. Through him, Harrison explores the limits of good intentions within systems of inequality. Errol’s willingness to change, to “move to Mooroopna,” and adopt Indigenous expressions (‘snotty googles’, p.191) suggests that reconciliation begins not with assimilation but humility and empathy.

Leon (Cousin) Key Quote: “She’s no lady, she’s just a little –” (to Errol, referring to Dolly, p.167) Leon associates with the rough ‘cork-tree lads’ who treat women as objects. His violence toward Dolly reflects both personal corruption and societal neglect. Nan’s comment that the boys have ‘had it hard’ (p.139) hints at systemic roots — poor education, limited work, and widespread alcoholism. Mentioned as a ‘motherless child’ (p.131), Leon represents a generation damaged by dispossession and lack of support.

Analysis Leon embodies the tragic consequences of internalised oppression and the failures of state policy. While his assault on Dolly is unforgivable, his existence indicts the colonial system that left many young Indigenous men alienated, impoverished, and angry. He is both victimiser and victim — a figure who underscores the intergenerational damage caused by racism and inequality.

Papa Dear Key Quote: “That’s how important [the Queen] is – getting a meeting with the busiest Aboriginal around!” (Nan, referring to Papa Dear, p.128) Papa Dear never appears on stage but looms large through others’ stories. A pastor who does ‘good work’ (p.143) and meets the Queen, he has gained status in white society, yet his activism yields little improvement for his people. His absence during family crises — floods, pregnancy, and poverty — highlights the emotional toll of political duty and the limits of symbolic representation.

Analysis Papa Dear symbolises a generation of Aboriginal leaders who sought reform through institutional acceptance rather than confrontation. While his work is well-intentioned, Harrison implies that activism divorced from community risks perpetuating absence rather than alleviating suffering. He is a figure of both hope and disillusionment.

The Inspector The Inspector is a smug bureaucrat who prides himself on “bringing change” (p.152), blind to the harm of assimilation. His hollow encouragement that the women should “take leadership” reveals a profound ignorance of their reality. His officiousness exemplifies paternalism — well-dressed oppression masquerading as progress.

Analysis The Inspector represents state surveillance and control — the polite face of systemic violence. Harrison uses his character to expose how government policy framed Aboriginal suffering as a problem to be managed rather than a wound to be healed.

Mr. Coody (The Rent Collector) Key Quote: “Your arrangements will need to be re-evaluated, with the impending new arrival.” (p.185) Mr. Coody’s condescension and moral judgment expose the hypocrisy of colonial authority. He condemns Dolly’s pregnancy and questions Nan’s decency, despite representing a system that denies Aboriginal families adequate housing. His “re-evaluation” of their tenancy embodies the state’s power to intrude upon private life.

Analysis Through Mr. Coody, Harrison critiques bureaucratic arrogance and the pretence of moral superiority that underpinned racist policies. Nan’s defiant act — throwing eggs at him — becomes symbolic rebellion, an assertion of dignity in the face of systemic degradation.

The Bank Manager Arrogant and self-satisfied, the Bank Manager embodies white patriarchal privilege. He refuses Gladys’s request for her daughter’s employment, patronises her with a vocabulary lesson, and reinforces racist stereotypes about Aboriginal unreliability (p.164).

Analysis He stands as a caricature of institutional prejudice — polite yet poisonous. Harrison uses him to illustrate how everyday racism operates behind the veneer of civility, undermining merit and silencing ambition.

Nancy Woolthorpe Key Quote: “Love your dress, Dolly. Love the fabric. My mother quite liked it too. When it was our sunroom curtains.” (p.166) Nancy is the archetypal ‘mean girl’ — wealthy, pretty, and cruel. She belittles Dolly at the ball, symbolising class snobbery and racial arrogance. Yet her hollow luxury contrasts sharply with the warmth and humour of Dolly’s family, suggesting that material comfort cannot replace love or community.

Analysis Nancy Woolthorpe serves as a foil to Dolly — shallow, privileged, and spiritually impoverished. Harrison’s satire of her behaviour underscores how racism is perpetuated not only through policy but through everyday social exclusion.

Ester and Her Boys Ester is a young Aboriginal mother living at Rumbalara with her three mischievous sons, and she is pregnant again. Her ‘whitefella husband’ (p.132) has given her a black eye, highlighting the cycles of violence and instability experienced by many Indigenous families of the time. When the Inspector visits, Nan fears that Ester’s children will be taken away (p.153) — and indeed, this fear becomes reality. Ester’s boys are removed, ostensibly for “assimilation”, but in truth to be institutionalised or adopted, separated from their family, community, and culture.

Analysis Ester’s brief appearance is a powerful allusion to the Stolen Generations. Her character encapsulates the vulnerability of Aboriginal mothers under the Assimilation Policy, whose children were taken “for their own good.” Harrison’s inclusion of Ester and her boys exposes the cruelty and hypocrisy of a government that dismantled families under the guise of progress. The boys’ removal functions as both a narrative tragedy and a historical indictment, reminding the audience that these policies perpetuated trauma across generations.

The Policeman The local policeman (the “Jungi”) appears following the devastating flood that destroys the women’s home and possessions. He exhibits little empathy for Dolly’s distress, and his irritation when she disobeys him reveals his insensitivity. Although he promises to protect their belongings, placing the encyclopaedias “high and dry,” he instead leaves them on the flooded ground (p.174), symbolising negligence and institutional indifference.

Analysis The policeman’s character demonstrates the absence of meaningful protection for Aboriginal people from authorities. His behaviour reflects the wider neglect of Indigenous welfare by institutions meant to safeguard all citizens. Harrison uses his actions as a metaphor for systemic disregard — the literal and figurative abandonment of Aboriginal people during times of crisis.

The Shire Councillors The Shire Councillors appear through a radio broadcast, discussing the “shanties” on Crown Land and claiming that Aboriginal families pose a sanitary risk to the town (p.180). Their rhetoric is filled with euphemisms and coded prejudice: they describe the Indigenous community as an “itinerant minority” while ignoring that these families are long-established residents. When Gladys interrupts their meeting to protest, the councillors order her ejection, branding her an “interloper” (p.181).

Analysis The councillors epitomise bureaucratic racism and the erasure of Aboriginal voices from public discourse. Their paternalistic tone and false concern for “public health” disguise the real motive of displacement and segregation. Gladys’s interruption becomes an act of defiance against a political structure that silences Indigenous women. Harrison’s portrayal of this scene transforms a moment of exclusion into one of empowerment, as Gladys reclaims her right to be heard.
Scene Summary and Analysis
Prologue: Aftermath

Summary In 1950s Australia, Nan Dear and Gladys rebuild their humpy (another word for a bush hut) after a flood. Gladys is Nan Dear’s middle-aged daughter and the mother of Dolly, a teenager. The women are Aboriginal Australians. Nan Dear and Gladys tear up Dolly’s magazines to repair the walls. Dolly returns home from school and almost protests when she sees her ruined magazines, but ultimately she says nothing, resigned. Nan Dear and Gladys hug Dolly.

Analysis The play introduces readers and viewers to its main characters as they rebuild their home. This establishes the home as something that must be tended to and maintained, and it also highlights the often invisible domestic labor that goes into maintaining a home. That labor requires sacrifices, like Dolly’s magazines, but the women are willing to put their personal wants aside for the sake of the family and their home.
Act 1, Scene 1 (A)

Summary Gladys listens to Queen Elizabeth on the radio and prepares to travel to town to see Queen Elizabeth, the first reigning monarch to visit Australia. Nan Dear dislikes the monarchy and pokes fun at Gladys for being a “loyal subject.” Dolly asks for help on a family tree she is making for school. Nan Dear is bewildered that Dolly is only supposed to list direct relatives and exclude her cousins. She evades Dolly’s questions about her great-grandparents and reminisces about her home by Murray River, which she was forced to leave. Gladys continues to make herself presentable for the Queen, and she fantasises about presenting a bouquet of flowers to Elizabeth before finding herself holding a handful of weeds.

Analysis Nan Dear disdains the British monarchy as the ultimate colonisers, who are responsible for the oppression of Aboriginal Australians on their own land. Gladys, on the other hand, is more assimilated into White society and thus shares White Australians’ excitement to see their queen. Nan Dear criticises her for this loyalty to a violently racist government. The school’s assumption that only direct relatives are significant enough to include on a family tree demonstrates how conceptions of family depend on culture. The school system is built around White Australian culture, which does not place the same value on extended family as Aboriginal culture. Meanwhile, Gladys loses herself in the fantasy of meeting Queen Elizabeth, and the sharp contrast between her imagined flowers and the reality of weeds suggests that Gladys indulges in fantasies as a form of escapism.
Act 1, Scene 1 (B)

Summary Gladys returns home after the Queen’s visit, exhausted. She explains to Nan Dear and Dolly that her taxi never arrived, leaving her to walk along the road hoping for a glimpse of the Queen. Gladys failed to see the Queen, though, because the roads have been lined with burlap to prevent Elizabeth from seeing the humpies along the riverbank. Gladys complains about the state of their housing, but Nan Dear insists that life is better than it was in the last days at Cummeragunja by the Murray River. Nan Dear tells Gladys that Papa Dear had a meeting with the Queen to discuss improving life for Aboriginal people.

Analysis The burlap-lined streets are a deliberate effort to keep Queen Elizabeth from seeing Aboriginal Australians and vice versa, and it indicates that the Australian government does not regard Aboriginal Australians as true Australian citizens. Through this racist mindset, Aboriginal Australians and their humpies are seen as an embarrassing reminder of the past on an otherwise modern and civilised land. Papa Dear’s role as a travelling activist introduces a gendered element to the racism that the family faces. Gladys, as a woman, is tied to her duties at home, so she can’t travel the country to fight for her people’s rights the way her father can.
Act 1, Scene 2 (A)

Summary Gladys listens to a radio quiz show and answers the questions along with the contestants, getting every one of them correct even as the contestants make mistakes. Dolly suggests Gladys try to become a contestant on the show, but Gladys laughs off the idea of an Aboriginal contestant. Nan Dear asks Dolly about a boy she was talking to. Dolly frustratedly explains that she won’t be marrying him because he is her cousin, as are most boys her age in the community. Gladys insists that Dolly won’t marry anyone until she finishes her studies, which prompts Dolly to ask how old Nan Dear was when she married. Nan Dear doesn’t answer. Dolly leaves to study, and Nan Dear questions Gladys for “put[ting] ideas” into Dolly’s head about the possibilities for her life. Nan Dear leaves to tend to a neighbour, Ester, who is pregnant and injured after an altercation with her abusive White husband. Gladys confesses she didn’t know about Ester’s predicament, and Nan Dear recommends Gladys pay less attention to the radio and more attention to the world around her.

Analysis Though Gladys fantasies about a better life, she is also well aware of the limitations she faces. She is familiar enough with racism to know that the radio quiz show would never accept her, despite her intelligence. Nan Dear believes optimism is dangerous, since she is convinced that the world will never improve enough for Dolly to have the kind of life Gladys dreams for her. At the same time, Nan Dear’s inquiry into the boys Dolly spends time with highlights that she is concerned about Dolly’s future in her own way. Ester’s situation demonstrates how gendered and racial dynamics play out within the home. As an Aboriginal woman, Ester has almost no power against the White man she is married to, with her only option being to turn to the members of her community for help.
Act 1 Scene 2 (B)

Summary A young White man, Errol Fisher, gets lost and ends up at the humpy. He encounters Dolly, and though the two don’t speak to each other, mutual attraction sparks between them. Errol then approaches Gladys and tries to sell her the encyclopedias he is peddling. Gladys turns him away at first, but she takes pity on Errol and allows him to present his rehearsed pitch. As Errol presents the encyclopedias, Gladys becomes interested in them, especially when Errol notes that a previous radio quiz show winner succeeded by studying an encyclopedia. He adds that encyclopedias are good for school-aged children, and Gladys has a fantasy of Dolly graduating in a cap and gown. Nan Dear enters and is skeptical of Errol and his encyclopedias. She asks what the books say about Aboriginal people, and Errol doesn’t know the answer. Gladys considers buying a set of encyclopedias, but Nan Dear stops her as Dolly comes in and helps Errol sort out his map. She explains that he should go to the White neighbourhood to sell his encyclopedias, and Gladys and Nan Dear agree. Errol departs, but he leaves his calling card, and Gladys is charmed by his manners and his descriptions of the encyclopedia. As Errol prepares to leave, Dolly asks him about his home in Melbourne and teases him for coming out to her family’s humpy in The Flats. She is confused and uncertain whether he returns her attraction to him. She helps him retrieve his bicycle from some local little boys, then returns to her family. Na

Analysis Errol is out of place in the humpies among The Flats, but he nervously recites the same rehearsed pitch he presents to potential White buyers. His sales pitch appeals to Gladys’s curiosity and thirst for knowledge as well as her desire for a better life for Dolly, and she once again loses herself in a fantasy sequence. Her imagined future for Dolly as a graduate seems as impossible as a dream, but Gladys believes in it. Nan Dear’s implication that the encyclopedias likely contain untrue and offensive information about Aboriginal people highlights how structures of knowledge and learning are not separate from the racism that informs other cultural institutions. Nan Dear, Dolly, and Gladys all agree that White people are the intended audience for encyclopedias, but Gladys is still tempted by the books’ promise of knowledge. Neither Dolly nor Errol are fully comfortable making conversation with someone of a different race, but Dolly finds herself enjoying Errol’s company and helps him when local boys see him as an easy target for theft. Her attraction to Errol leaves Dolly confused, and Nan Dear and Gladys’s differing reactions to Errol don’t help that confusion. Gladys is intrigued by Errol, while Nan Dear sees him as a threat.
Act 1, Scene 3

Summary Dolly rummages for linoleum at the town dump, where she fantasies about buying linoleum from an attractive salesman as a posh customer. Her fantasy fades and she returns home, passing a drunk cousin of hers (Leon) who invites her to a party. She turns him down and continues to the humpy, where Nan Dear scolds her for taking an unsafe route home. She scolds her further when Dolly refers derisively to her cousins who live along the route, insisting that “they’ve had it hard” but refusing to explain further. Errol returns to the humpy to see if Gladys wants to buy an encyclopedia set. Gladys is at work, so Errol invites himself to wait as an excuse to spend time with Dolly. She won’t let him inside the humpy, but they make conversation outside. After he unthinkingly makes a racist remark, he apologies and asks if Dolly wants to ride his bike with him. She gets on the bike by herself and rides around him, humming, and the two sing together. Dolly and Errol talk about their lives. Errol enjoys the freedom of being a travelling salesman, while Dolly has no sense of that freedom; she expects she will pick fruit or work at the cannery like everyone else in her community. When Errol asks what Dolly dreams of doing, she muses that she would like to be a model or a nurse, but she knows her mother has other dreams for her. Gladys arrives with a bookshelf from the dump, and, not noticing Dolly’s embarrassment, explains to Errol that the landfill plot is a desirable piece of land a

Analysis Like her mother, Dolly escapes into fantasy sequences to imagine a world where more possibilities are open to her. Nan Dear, on the other hand, remains unwaveringly practical. She both sympathies with Dolly’s cousins and recognises some of them (such as Leon) as a threat to Dolly’s safety. Nan Dear believes in the importance of family, and that demands empathy, even for relatives she dislikes. Errol likes Dolly, but he was born and raised in a culture that fundamentally disrespects her and her culture. As such, he carries racist biases that he must consciously try to unlearn. Dolly, for her part, forgives Errol’s casual racism and opens herself up to enjoying her time with him, seemingly in part because he tries to shed his racist biases. The freedom that Errol enjoys, and Dolly’s lack of that same freedom, comes from both racism and sexism. The racism of Australian society prevents Dolly and her community from holding any kind of job besides manual labour, while sexist expectations of a woman’s role in the home keep Dolly tied to her house and unable to travel like Errol can. Dolly is embarrassed that Errol knows her family gets furniture from the dump. She is already aware that, according to the commonplace racism in their society, she and her family are of a lower status than Errol, and their secondhand furniture is a symbol of that to her. Act 1 Scene 4

Summary Dolly sets the table as Gladys serves dinner. Nan Dear complains that they haven’t set a place for Papa Dear. Dolly and Gladys point out that Papa Dear has been preaching across Western Australia for three months, but Dolly sets a place for him anyway. In a fantasy sequence, Papa Dear returns home and kisses Gladys on the top of the head before disappearing. Dolly tells Gladys and Nan Dear about a job as a cashier that she hopes to get, but Nan Dear dismisses this and declares that Dolly will continue to work with them at the cannery. She sends Dolly out of the room and tells Gladys that a store in town will never hire an Aboriginal girl from The Flats.

Analysis Gladys’s fantasy sequence about Papa Dear makes clear that she shares Nan Dear’s belief in the importance of family. Gladys wants her family to be reunited, and she wants her father’s affection. This emphasises the family dynamics present in the play; Gladys is a mother, but she is also a daughter, and she misses her father. Gladys and Nan Dear continue to butt heads over Dolly’s future. Nan Dear sending Dolly out of the room reveals that even though Nan Dear previously chastised Gladys for giving Dolly false hope for the future, Nan Dear doesn’t want Dolly to hear how limited Nan Dear believes her prospects really are.
Act 1, Scene 5

Summary Errol is in the humpy, much to Dolly’s embarrassment, as he sells Gladys an encyclopedia set to be paid for in instalments. She recounts earning money by picking fruit and remembers Dolly sleeping peacefully as a baby among the carpet snakes in the tree paddocks. Errol asks if the snakes hurt young Dolly, and the women are confused by the question since carpet snakes are harmless. Errol gives Gladys Volume A of the encyclopedia, and she displays it proudly on her new bookcase. Dolly talks to Errol outside the humpy, and he asks why the family lives in The Flats. As Dolly replies that Nan Dear and Gladys like to live among their community, Gladys steps outside and gestures encouragingly to Errol. He stammers nervously until Gladys tells Dolly that Errol wants to invite her to a dance in town. Dolly refuses, knowing that the townspeople won’t accept Errol bringing an Aboriginal girl from The Flats as a date, but Errol insists he doesn’t care. Dolly cautiously agrees to the date before Nan Dear comes outside and sternly tells Errol to leave.

Analysis Errol’s misunderstanding of the story about the carpet snake emphasises how removed he is from the cultural context that informs the way Gladys is telling the story. Errol’s cultural context is expressed in the encyclopedias rather than through oral stories or practical knowledge, and it is positioned as the default culture that Aboriginal Australians must assimilate to. The family’s decision to remain in The Flats despite the constant flooding highlights how home, for these women, is not only a matter of physical shelter but also of community. Gladys continues to extend her approval of Errol by encouraging the relationship between him and Dolly, while Nan Dear continues to make clear that she does not want to include Errol in their community. The abuse the neighbour Ester experiences at her White husband’s hands perhaps informs Nan Dear’s opinion.
Act 1, Scene 6

Summary A White Inspector surveys the living conditions in the humpy, making strained conversation with an anxious Gladys and Nan Dear. He is a representative of the government of Victoria, making a report on how Aboriginal people live, and he patronisingly tells Gladys and Nan Dear that they must assimilate to White culture. Dolly enters and the Inspector sees her before Gladys and Nan Dear can shoo her away. After he leaves, Gladys and Nan Dear discuss the possibility of the government taking Dolly away.

Analysis Gladys and Nan Dear’s fear of the Inspector comes from his status as a representative of the Aborigines Protection Board, which held absolute authority over the Aboriginal population of New South Wales. It had, among other privileges, the right to displace Aboriginal people from their homes and the right to remove Aboriginal children from their families. Gladys and Nan Dear are keenly aware that the Inspector has the power to break their family apart, which is why they try to shoo Dolly away before he can see her.
Act 1, Scene 7

Summary Gladys gets dressed for a fundraiser. Gladys’s uncle intends to raise money to negotiate for Daish’s, the dump, in order to build Aboriginal housing. He plans to call the housing Rumbalara, which means “end of the rainbow.” Meanwhile, Dolly gets dressed for her date with Errol, and Nan Dear and Gladys are astonished at how beautiful she looks. When Nan Dear learns that Dolly is going out with Errol, however, she pretends to have a coughing fit, and Dolly cancels her plans to tend to Nan Dear.

Analysis Though the local government denied Daish’s to the Aboriginal community, the community continues to fight for it and for access to stable housing. Meanwhile, Nan Dear makes clear her distrust of Errol and her dislike of his burgeoning relationship with Dolly. Nan Dear believes that a relationship with a White boy will endanger Dolly, so she keeps her from the dance in an attempt to protect her granddaughter.
Act 1, Scene 8

Summary Dolly returns from school to find Nan Dear doing the laundry. Dolly reports that Ester’s sons were not at school, and that everyone suspects it’s because of the Inspector. Gladys gets home and tells Dolly about a trainee program she heard about, and Dolly is frustrated that her mother wants her to act like a White person. Gladys asserts that she wants for Dolly what any mother wants. Dolly asks Nan Dear why they can’t have “a normal life,” to which Nan Dear responds that their life is normal and there is no point in trying to change it. They discuss the upcoming Miss Marroopna-Shepparton Ball, and Nan Dear promises that she will make Dolly a new dress for the occasion.

Analysis Ester once again represents the level of powerlessness that Aboriginal women, as doubly marginalised people, must contend with. The government takes away her children, robbing her of her status as a mother. Gladys tries to be the mother Dolly needs, but Dolly is frustrated that her mother is teaching her to get by in White society rather than how to thrive in Aboriginal society. Dolly faces the constant expectation to assimilate into White culture, which makes her resentful of her own Aboriginal identity. Nan Dear encourages her to embrace her identity and traditions, but she does this by denying that any other kind of life is possible.
Act 1, Scene 9

Summary Gladys talks to Dolly about the latest book of the encyclopedia to arrive. She tells Dolly that Errol asked about her and encourages Dolly to go to the ball with him. The conversation gets tense as Gladys urges Dolly “not to let them shame you.” Dolly counters that Gladys should take her own advice. Nan Dear enters, and Gladys asserts to her that Gladys is Dolly’s mother, and she wants Errol to walk Dolly home from the ball.

Analysis Gladys and Dolly both struggle with the conflicting impulses to assimilate into White society and to honor their Aboriginal identities. This tension is complicated by the mother-daughter relationships at play. In this scene, Dolly stands up to Gladys while Gladys does the same to Nan Dear.
Act 1, Scene 10

Summary Gladys speaks to a bank manager and asks him to hire Dolly as a teller. Elsewhere, Dolly picks fruit and sings to herself. The bank manager questions Dolly’s qualifications and continues to come up with reasons to deny her the job even as Gladys rebuts each of his points. Gladys remembers Dolly asking if she lets White people shame her, and Gladys insists that the bank manager offer Dolly a job. He agrees to interview Dolly.

Analysis Gladys has high hopes for Dolly’s future, and she sets out to make those dreams a reality by visiting the bank and confronting the manager. Her desire to do right by Dolly inspires Gladys to voice her frustration with the racist systems around her, and she is further empowered by her belief in her and her family’s capability to make a better world for themselves.
Act 1, Scene 11

Summary Dolly dances with Errol at the ball. She is uncomfortably aware that the White partygoers are staring at them, but Errol comforts her, and she imagines winning the title of Miss Mooroopna-Shepparton Ball. Instead, the winner is a White girl who mocks Dolly for wearing a dress made of fabric scrounged from the dump. Dolly, humiliated, flees the dance and is confronted by her drunk cousin Leon. To punish Dolly for acting as if she is too good for her community, Leon attacks her and Errol, tearing Dolly’s dress and leaving Errol with a black eye.

Analysis Dolly is uncomfortable and conscious of the danger around her, but she lets herself dream about a world in which racism wouldn’t prevent her from winning Miss Mooroopna-Shepparton Ball. Though the play usually depicts this kind of daydreaming as an act of protective optimism, in this case, Dolly’s hope for a better world is cruelly punished by humiliation and violence.
Act 1, Scene 12
Summary At the humpy, Gladys thinks happily of how much fun Dolly must be having. However, Nan Dear senses that something is wrong.

Analysis Just as the violence that ensued after Dolly’s fantasy sequence shows the hazards of losing oneself in imagination, Nan Dear now demonstrates that her cynicism and wariness are sometimes more realistic than Gladys’s optimism.
Act 1, Scene 13

Summary Errol finds Dolly crying at the river, and she helps treat his black eye. Though her dress is ruined, Errol reassures Dolly that she is still beautiful. They tell each other about their families, and Dolly explains that she grew up with three older brothers and 27 male cousins, which taught her how to fight. Errol marvels at her stories of her family. His father is strict and traditional, and he insists that Errol’s mother is “too delicate” to get a job. Errol and Dolly get closer, but Dolly pulls away, unwilling to subject either of them to the trials of an interracial relationship. Errol counters that the two of them could move to the city together for a better world. But Dolly argues that Errol’s version of a better world is different from hers; her priority is staying with her family, since that is what home means to her. They fight, but she definitively turns him down and at last Errol accepts this. Dolly starts to walk home, but she is intercepted by Leon.

Analysis Errol and Dolly have grown up with vastly different conceptions of family, both of which are shaped by their race and culture. Errol’s version of family is the strict nuclear family that is prioritized and rewarded by White Australian culture. He was born into his family but has no real attachment to it. Dolly, on the other hand, has meaningful connections with her entire extended family. She is also used to manual labor being expected of everyone, including women, while Errol’s family embodies a kind of White middle-class gender roles that forbid women from working. Errol’s unthinking and casual racism has not yet hindered his relationship with Dolly because she knows he is well-meaning. However, it proves the undoing of their relationship as Errol can’t wrap his head around the fact that Dolly doesn’t want to leave her community to live an assimilated life with him in the city. She is also more aware of the violence interracial couples face, since she is generally more aware of racism than Errol.
Act 1, Scene 14

Summary Nan Dear and Gladys pack up the humpy, preparing to evacuate for a flood. They worry about Dolly, who is still not home. A jungi (policeman) arrives to escort them to the dump, which is the evacuation site since it is above the flood line. They are about to leave when Dolly arrives, clearly traumatised. She “half screams” when the jungi tries to touch her, and she refuses to answer when Gladys and Nan Dear ask her what happened.

Analysis The flood mirrors Dolly’s implied rape by her cousin Leon. The flood will destroy the humpy, emphasising the instability of Dolly’s home. Meanwhile, Leon’s assault destabilises not only Dolly herself but also her connections to her community. Her trauma is obvious to those around her, but she refuses to share the truth with Gladys and Nan Dear, with whom she is usually so open.
Act 2, Scene 1

Summary The women return to the humpy after the flood. Dolly, still “shell-shocked,” continues to wear the dress she wore to the ball, which is now badly torn and muddied. Nan Dear and Gladys clean up the wreckage, and Gladys finds that all her encyclopedias are ruined. She insists she doesn’t mind. Nan Dear muses that moving to the Rumbalara housing development might be a good idea for Dolly. Nan Dear and Gladys decide not to tell Papa Dear what happened to Dolly, since it’s “women’s business.” Errol arrives to apologise to Dolly, but Nan Dear and Gladys violently drive him away, assuming that he is the one who assaulted Dolly. Errol is devastated that they could think him capable of such a thing, and after he leaves, Dolly finally speaks, confirming that Errol was not the one who raped her.

Analysis Dolly in unable to move past the horror of being assaulted, and her refusal to change her dress reflects how stuck she is in the moment of her trauma. Nan Dear and Gladys, meanwhile, try to rebuild both Dolly and the humpy. The family lives in The Flats to be with their family and community, but they are prepared to move to Rumbalara now that Nan Dear believes Rumbalara might help Dolly. Nan Dear and Gladys agree that the attack on Dolly, and the work of helping her recover, are “women’s business,” which reflects that Papa Dear might be an important activist, but he is not privy to the equally important work that goes on in his own home. Gladys’s affection for Errol is gone now that she suspects him of hurting her daughter, which emphasises how committed she is to protecting Dolly. Dolly, on the other hand, retains some loyalty to Errol, breaking her silence to make sure her family knows that he is innocent.
Act 2, Scene 2

Summary A bulldozer destroys the family’s humpy, and they move into a sterile, small, and unwelcoming concrete house at Rumbalara. Nan Dear develops a cough, but she promises to make curtains for the new house.

Analysis The family leaves behind the humpy, which was unstable but familiar and made by the women themselves, for the distinctly unhomelike Rumbalara housing. The contrast between the humpy and Rumbalara illuminates the injustice of needing to choose between safe housing and emotionally fulfilling housing. Nan Dear’s promise to liven up the house with curtains also underscores the importance of domestic labour in making a house a home.
Act 2, Scene 3

Summary Gladys brings Dolly a letter from the bank, but Dolly is uninterested in the job as teller. She tells her mother that this job is Gladys’s dream, not Dolly’s, and angrily suggests Gladys “fix [her] own house.” Gladys storms out. Nan Dear pays the rent to the rent collector Mr. Coody and helps Dolly prepare a meal. They listen to the radio and are surprised to hear Gladys arguing with city councilors about building Aboriginal housing at Daish’s. Gladys is removed from the council meeting, but Dolly and Nan Dear are still proud of her.

Analysis Though the family has left the humpy, the image of rebuilding a house returns as Dolly uses a person’s house as a symbol for their life. This metaphor emphasises the importance Dolly and her family place on home. It also signifies how the sterile Rumbalara housing is causing fractures within the home, which was previously a space where the family is united. Mr. Coody’s arrival to collect rent makes clear that the family’s home is not their own anymore; it is owned and controlled by Mr. Coody. In light of this lack of control, Gladys asserts her dignity and a level of control by refusing to let the city councillors debate her life without her input.
Act 2, Scene 4

Summary Errol finds Gladys cutting wood outside the Rumbalara housing. They discuss the encyclopedias. Gladys doesn’t want to buy any more sets of encyclopedias, and even though she isn’t supposed to be able to cancel her contract, Errol offers to help her do so. Gladys reveals she can’t read or write, so Errol helps her compose the required letter. Gladys apologies for assuming that Errol raped Dolly, and Errol asks if he can see Dolly. Gladys gently but firmly tells him that Dolly won’t want to see him. Gladys asks Errol to teach her to read and write. She explains that Nan Dear attended school when the family lived at Cummeragunja, but the mission managers were cruel, and Gladys was sent off at a young age to do domestic work for a large wealthy family. After that, she married Dolly’s father and was too busy working and raising children to learn. Errol agrees to teach Gladys to read. He still hasn’t given up hope of reuniting with Dolly, and he hopes to prove that he “can be worthy of her.”

Analysis Gladys has always liked Errol as a boy who might be good for Dolly, but now their relationship begins to develop on its own terms. She priorities Dolly’s emotional well being over Errol’s desire to see her, but she nonetheless treats Errol with gentle respect. Gladys is the kind of strong and caring mother that Errol never had, and she allows herself to be vulnerable with him when she reveals she can’t read or write. The reasons behind Gladys’s illiteracy are a combination of racism and sexism. She didn’t learn to read as a child because the Aborigines Protection Board removed her from her family, who were already being mistreated at Cummeragunja. By the time she was old enough to escape the most controlling parts of the Aborigines Protection Board, Gladys found herself where she is now––bound by the domestic responsibilities expected of her as a woman.
Act 2, Scene 5

Summary The rent collector Mr. Coody comes by for the rent and finds Nan Dear cooking eggs for a heavily pregnant Dolly. He notes that he will re-evaluate their lease in light of Dolly’s pregnancy, but Nan Dear takes him to task for consistently invading his tenants’ privacy and imposing too many rules. She throws eggs at him, and he flees. Dolly and Nan Dear look at Dolly’s old family tree assignment, and Dolly shares that she plans to name her baby after Papa Dear. She confesses that she is worried she won’t love the baby. Nan Dear says that Dolly is finally a woman and thus ready to hear the truth: Gladys’s biological father is a White man who raped Nan Dear when she was a teenager. This is why Nan Dear has been so opposed to Dolly’s relationship with Errol––especially because Errol has the same surname as Nan Dear’s attacker. When Dolly shares that she broke things off with Errol because he wants to take her away from her family, Nan Dear encourages her to consider leaving. At the same time, she insists that Dolly mustn’t resume her relationship with Errol, and Dolly agrees.

Analysis Mr. Coody represents how White authority infringes on every moment of Aboriginal life by denying Aboriginal people privacy and self-determination within their own homes. Nan Dear resists this facet of White supremacy, asserting her home as a space she and her family have the right to control. While Dolly’s belief in the importance of family led to tension with Errol, it connects her to Nan Dear, who believes the same thing. Dolly’s decision to name the baby after Papa Dear reaffirms her devotion to her family and her desire to honor her relative’s traditions and legacy. Nan Dear values tradition as well, but she also wants Dolly to make a meaningful future for herself, which is why she encourages Dolly to leave. Nan Dear’s dislike of Errol is also explained as not simply a general protectiveness of her granddaughter but a reaction to trauma that she now chooses to share with Dolly in a moment of honesty and vulnerability.
Act 2, Scene 6

Summary Errol is teaching Gladys to read, and they practice on the Inspector’s report of Aboriginal living conditions. Gladys encourages Errol to call her “Aunty” and asks about his feelings for Dolly. He admits that he still has feelings for her, but he doesn’t know what she wants. Gladys invites Errol to a public meeting in Melbourne the following week, where Papa Dear will be speaking on Aboriginal housing. He accepts the invitation, and Gladys tells Errol that even if he and Dolly don’t end up together, she likes Errol.

Analysis Errol loves Dolly, but that love has been hindered by a lack of understanding of where she comes from. Now Errol is developing a love for Dolly’s family and an appreciation for her community that mirror Dolly’s own loyalty to her loved ones. Gladys’s request that Errol call her “Aunty” is an invitation to join the family, which demonstrates that her understanding of family is inclusive and based on personal bonds. This contrasts Errol’s previous understanding of family as something constraining and dependent only on blood ties.
Act 2, Scene 7

Summary Nan Dear, Gladys, and Dolly sit in the audience at the public meeting. With them is Dolly’s baby, Regina. Dolly says that Gladys should be giving a speech, but Nan Dear scoffs that speechmaking is “men’s business.” Dolly gave up on the bank interview, but she has been awarded a scholarship to a nursing program in Melbourne, and Gladys is thrilled for her. Errol arrives and sits with the women. Gladys pulls Nan Dear away to go find Papa Dear. Errol is startled to see Regina, and he finally realises what happened the night of the flood. He apologies to Dolly “for everything,” and she apologies in turn. He offers to move to the country so they can be together and stay near Dolly’s family, but she tells him that she is going to Melbourne and not ready for a romantic relationship. He respects her decision, and they agree to remain friends. Nan Dear returns, feeling ill, and Dolly hurries off to get her medicine. Errol offers to drive her to the hospital, and when she refuses, he offers to drive her home. She is touched. Nan Dear asks about Errol’s surname, and he explains that his parents adopted it after immigrating from Germany. Nan Dear is relieved that Errol is not related to the Fisher who once assaulted her. She kisses Errol on the cheek. Dolly sees this and is stunned. Papa Dear fails to arrive to present the petition, so Gladys does it in his stead. She makes a speech petitioning Queen Elizabeth to give Aboriginal Australians decent housing and to finally treat the

Analysis Dolly has grown up from a girl to a woman. She has chosen a future for herself and will be leaving the protection of her mother and grandmother, while she herself is tasked with raising the family’s next generation. Her family has faith in her ability to pursue a career, but Nan Dear still believes that woman and men’s work are divided categories, and that public activism belongs to men. Errol makes clear his devotion to Dolly in this scene. He unequivocally apologies, recognising his own wrongdoing, and promises to prioritise Dolly’s needs over his own wants. His desire for a relationship is not hindered at all by Dolly’s baby, which emphasises that he is ready to commit not only to Dolly but to her family. When Dolly turns him down, he respects her ability to choose what she wants and is just glad to stay in her life as a friend. Errol proves to Nan Dear that despite his occasional moments of unthinking racism, he is fundamentally kind and respectful. However, she cannot let herself like him until she knows for certain that he isn’t related to her rapist. When she learns that Errol has no connection to her past trauma, Nan Dear embraces him as a good choice for Dolly. Papa Dear’s work is important, as his speech provides Gladys the foundation to begin her public activism. t: Sample 1 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay
b: Sample 1 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay Topic: How does Rainbow’s End suggest that everything will be alright? In the 2005 Indigenous play ‘Rainbows End’, playwright Jane Harrison suggests everything will be alright for the Dear family, despite the challenges they combat. This is shown through the portrayal of self-determination, coupled with the continued hope and optimism of the Dears. However, the heavily damaging colonial attitudes to the Indigenous community act as a constant threat looming over the community and their futures. Throughout the play, Harrison highlights the unwavering self-determination of the Indigenous community, suggesting everything will be alright due to the strong resolve of the Dear family. This is depicted most vividly through the growth of Gladys, initially reserved and acquiescent, but steadily becoming more outspoken and steadfast in her beliefs for a better future. This culminates in Gladys taking a bold stand for the indigenous community at the town meeting, stating “I’m not an interloper – I belong here! - this is my land!”. Gladys’ open defiance in the meeting, challenging cultural norms and expectations, acts as a pivotal switch from Gladys’ belief everything will be alright, to taking direct action in pursuit of a better future for her family. Similarly, Dolly’s stoicism and resilience in the face of adversity and her assault serve as an emphatic representation of selfdetermination. After her assault, Dolly’s honest and true nature in

t: Sample 2 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay
b: Sample 2 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay Topic: ’Harrison’s play celebrates characters who show strength through adversity.’ Discuss. Set in Victorian town, Shepparton, Jane Harrison’s 2007 play, Rainbow’s End, explores the lives of three generations of Koori women as they navigate the setbacks and challenges faced by First Nations people in 1950s Victoria. At a time where the government regulates the policies of Assimilation of the Indigenous community, these women are vulnerable to the impacts of racism, colonisation and suppression of knowledge. But despite facing discrimination at every turn, Harrison commemorates their resilience in the face of adversity. The women are able to become symbols of hope as they refuse to allow their struggles to define them. Throughout Rainbow’s End, the struggle for the Indigenous community to pursue their dreams due to racial prejudice is highlighted in order to spotlight their ability to overcome it. This is demonstrated in scene ten in the bank managers office. Gladys tries to apply Dolly for a job as the bank teller, assuring the bank manager that she is fit for the job by advocating for her brains and wits. But despite Dolly being a good candidate, the manager is hesitant about letting her apply. He argues that, “reliability is important,” implying that Dolly is unreliable despite never having met her. He makes this assumption due to his personal prejudice to people of Dolly’s heritage. This demonstrates the racial discrimination th

t: Sample 3 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay
b: Sample 3 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay Topic: ‘Least here we do things our way- no one breathin’ down our necks’ Home is central to identity in Rainbow’s End. To what extent do you agree? Amongst the racism and oppression, it is clear that ‘home’ is of the utmost importance to the identity of Harrison’s Indigenous characters. Set against a backdrop of white Australian superiority in the 1950s, ‘Rainbow’s End’ by Jane Harrison explores the central theme of ‘home’ in the context of identity. For the three generational main characters, their ‘humpy’ alongside the riverbank of the Mooroopna flats is integral to the make up of who they are and essential for Harrison to showcase the strength and resilience needed for First Nations to fight their marginalised status. Beyond the physical ‘home’, identity is shaped by the family and culture surrounding the characters through the lens of white and Indigenous Australians. Throughout the first part of the play, ‘home’ is physically represented as the ‘humpy’, a hut with a dirt floor and hessian-covered walls. Despite the poverty of the main character’s living conditions, it isn’t the place where they sleep that is important to them as much as the culture surrounding them. For the character of Nan, "the Murray…Is your place isn’t it” due to the immense sense of belonging she feels after her oppressive experience with the Cummeragunja mission and the close proximity of her Aboriginal ‘family.’ In this way Harrison highlights the role

t: Sample 4 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay
b: Sample 4 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay Topic: Harrison’s play celebrates characters who show strength through adversity. Discuss. Jane Harrison’s play Rainbows End is a powerful exploration of the lives of three generations of Indigenous women, Nan Dear, her daughter Gladys, and granddaughter Dolly, in 1950s rural Australia. Harrison presents their daily struggles with poverty, limited access to education, and systemic racism, not to victimise them, but to celebrate their resilience and determination. Through her portrayal of these women, Harrison critiques the entrenched hierarchies that marginalise Indigenous communities, while affirming the strength required to survive and resist. Ultimately, the play celebrates the capacity of Indigenous women to endure adversity, even as it recognises the costs of their survival. Harrison portrays how racial discrimination restricts Indigenous Australians’ opportunities and dreams. This is evident in Scene Ten, when Gladys visits the bank in the hope of securing Dolly a position as a teller. Gladys proudly emphasises her daughter’s academic success, noting that Dolly has completed her “leaving certificate” and is “top of her class,” in an attempt to counteract the stereotypes imposed upon Indigenous people. However, the bank manager dismisses Dolly’s achievements, questioning how she would “fit in” and whether she could be “relied on” to attend work daily, despite never having met her.
