t: Sample 5 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay
b: Sample 5 - High Scoring Rainbow’s End Essay Topic: ’ Rainbow’s End is a bitter condemnation of colonisation.’ Discuss. Cemented within a world enveloped by overpowering white culture, Rainbows End seeks to explore the lengths of condemnation expressed by Indigenous Australians, juxtaposed with the desire to be included. Within the play, Harrison blatantly reveals consistent resistance and dislike aimed towards white authority yet contrasts this with younger generations who desire integration and inclusion in society. By depicting Indigenous women in this way, Harrison can characterise older generations as resistant to white knowledge and eternally furious at the erasure of Indigenous culture yet depicts younger characters as open to colonisation to succeed in their corrupted society. Overall, Harrison criticises the unjust dilution of Indigenous culture, simultaneously advocating for Indigenous Australians to continue fighting to be heard over white dominance. Harrison calculatingly embeds Indigenous resistance throughout the play, through unwavering denial, which represents a resilience to maintain Indigenous culture, despite attempts to silence it. Harrison evidence this though Nan Dear showing her despise for Errol’s encyclopedias, asking him what it says, “in that there encyclops about Aboriginals”. Harrison’s lexical choice of characterising Nan through her informal and grammatically incorrect language of “that there” and “encyclops”, illustrates Nan’s refusal to submit

Introduction From the very beginning of your schooling career, you have been introduced to several different methods of writing essays. Although all structures have their advantages and disadvantages, it is essential that you have a fool-proof method of writing essays for your end of year examination. Having a solid method of writing analytical essays and practicing these throughout the year will result in a strong foundation, enabling you to tackle any essay question with confidence. The key to success lies not only in understanding the content but also in being able to present your ideas in a clear, structured, and coherent manner. With a well-practised approach, you will be able to break down complex texts, analyse them effectively, and construct a well-supported argument that demonstrates both depth and clarity of thought. In the following, we will explore a structured approach to writing an analytical text response. By familiarising yourself with this framework, you will be better equipped to organise your ideas, manage your time efficiently, and ultimately maximise your performance in the exam.

t: What is Needed in an Essay
b: What is Needed in an Essay An essay is a structured piece of writing that presents an argument, analysis, or narrative on a particular topic. The purpose of an essay is to communicate a clear, well-reasoned point of view, often backed by evidence and examples. Essays are typically composed of several key parts: an introduction, body paragraphs, and a conclusion. Each section plays a specific role in conveying the writer’s ideas and helping the reader understand and engage with the topic at hand.

Introduction The introduction serves as the starting point of the essay, setting the stage for the discussion to follow. It introduces the topic, provides necessary background information, and presents the thesis statement, which outlines the main argument or purpose of the essay. A strong introduction grabs the reader’s attention, often through a compelling hook, and establishes the importance of the topic. It should also provide a clear roadmap of what the essay will cover, guiding the reader’s expectations and providing a sense of structure.

Body Paragraphs The body of an essay is where the main arguments or points are developed and supported with evidence, examples, or analysis. Typically, an essay contains three body paragraphs, although this can vary depending on the length and depth of the topic. Each paragraph should focus on a single aspect of the thesis statement and contribute to the overall argument.

Conclusion The conclusion is the final part of the essay, where the writer sums up the key points made in the body paragraphs and reinforces the thesis statement. It provides closure to the discussion and leaves a lasting impression on the reader. A strong conclusion restates the main argument in a fresh way, summarising the key insights presented in the essay. It may also offer suggestions for further exploration or suggest the broader implications of the topic.
Planning

Deconstructing the Prompt When first planning your essay, you must directly respond to the prompt that is presented to you, and consider the key authorial messages that could help you respond with insight. This involves three important steps: Find the key concepts. This will most likely correlate with the key concepts you have studied throughout the unit, you should also make sure to identify synonyms of these concepts too! Find any instructional verbs. These are words that instruct you to answer the prompt in a certain manner, in English the main verbs to look out for is discuss, do you agree, to what extent or how. Identify any expectations/limitations. These ore words that limit what you con include in your essays and set on expectation on what you should be discussing. Example prompt: ‘The dream sequences in Rainbow’s End are the characters’ only escape from the depressing reality of life.’ Do you agree? This is a very straightforward prompt—you need to demonstrate a multiple-sided response to what Harrison perceives as the role of dreams and aspirations in her characters’ lives. There are no limits to what should be discussed here, but you are expected to focus your discussion on the ideas of dreams, escape, and reality as they relate to the values, identities, and choices of Nan Dear, Gladys, and Dolly. Consider the historical context of 1950s assimilationist Australia, racism, class, and the characters’ evolving sense of agency throughout the play.

Contention Now that you understand what the prompt is asking, we can begin to think about what our interpretation of Harrison’s message is. Let’s consider the first prompt again: ‘The dream sequences in Rainbow’s End are the characters’ only escape from the depressing reality of life.’ Do you agree? We can approach this in multiple ways. Here are three simplistic interpretations (agree, disagree, somewhere in between): Harrison suggests that dreams offer the characters comfort, but they cannot change the reality of their circumstances. Harrison implies that dreams are the characters’ only refuge from a society that denies them dignity and opportunity. Harrison demonstrates that it is action, not dreaming, that ultimately allows the characters to escape their circumstances. However, these interpretations are quite general and lack specificity. They don’t tell the reader exactly what you are arguing. Let’s improve them with more precise language and deeper knowledge of the text: Harrison portrays dreams as both sustaining and limiting, suggesting that Gladys’s fantasies of meeting the Queen preserve her dignity while simultaneously revealing how deeply colonial ideals of acceptance have been internalised. Harrison implies that, for a family denied housing, education, and respect, the imagination becomes a vital site of survival and self-worth in a society that offers no material escape. Harrison critiques passive dreaming by charting Gladys’s transformation from daydreamer to a

Extending Sentences - Connectives To create a ’sophisticated’ idea you need to avoid single ideas, all of the contentions above are singular and lack connections to other ideas. We can connect them together using three simple connectives (and their advanced synonyms!) Useful Conjunctions and Their Purposes Conjunction Purpose Synonyms Because This conjunction explains why the information you have stated is true, demonstrating greater understanding of the author’s purpose. As, as a result of, considering, being, for, through, seeing as. But This conjunction adds contradictory information, allowing for greater debate. However, nevertheless, yet, despite, even so, nonetheless, except. So This conjunction allows for inferencing the cause and effect of the statement, which demonstrates greater thinking and ideas about the text. Therefore, consequently, accordingly, leading to. From here, we can take these singular idea contentions and extend them with these connectives in order to make multi-idea contentions that can create sophisticated links between ideas:
Topic Sentences Now we can create topic sentences that allow to explore complex ideas linked using BECAUSE, BUT & so.

t: Authorial Message/Intent - NOT PLOT OR CHAPTER!
b: Authorial Message/Intent - NOT PLOT OR CHAPTER! The sentence should be focused on an authorial message, not just a concept or character­ make the author active -the most common start is the author + verb: E.g Harrison explores/communicates/illustrates etc.

Authorial Verbs Analytical Verbs for Text Analysis Conveys Explores Demonstrates Emphasises Communicates Depicts Illustrates Challenges Identifies Frames Reveals Highlights States Implies Suggests Underlines

Prepositional Phrases Another way to structure this sentence is to use a prepositional phrase at the beginning of the sentence that introduces ideas to vary your sentence structure: Throughout the text, Harrison explores.. Textual Analysis Phrases and Sentence Starters Throughout By (authorial verb) In keeping with With an eye to Through From the outset In the absence of Under the By Beyond the Within the To the extent During Concerning the Without

Specificity and Relevancy Examiners punish vague or general statements, especially those that lack clear links to the topic or the author’s message. For example: By emphasising the importance of dreams in the play, Harrison shows that they matter to Gladys. This is not technically incorrect—but it is far too general and superficial. It doesn’t demonstrate insightful analysis, nor does it show a clear understanding of Harrison’s authorial intent. This kind of vague sentence would not receive full marks. Here’s an improved version: By emphasising Gladys’s fantasies of royal recognition, Harrison portrays how dreams function not as an escape from reality, but as a fragile shield of dignity for those denied respect and opportunity by an assimilationist society. This is better. It is clear, specific, well-structured, and shows awareness of both the theme (dreams vs reality), character motivation, and authorial message. It also directly links to the contention: that dreams sustain the characters, even in circumstances that offer no material escape. The issue you will face is that there is only so much you can discuss in your paragraph from here, as you have only examined one idea—dreams sustain dignity. To fix this, lets’ go back to an old friend:

t: Because, But, So - Make a Complex Sentence!
b: Because, But, So - Make a Complex Sentence! Too often, students fail to complete long paragraphs because they are stuck repeating themselves or have run out of things to say about their topic they introduced. Therefore, if we have two ideas in our topic sentence, we can ’hinge’ these sentences together which demonstrate complex understanding, writing and structure - all of them are key parts of the rubric you are judged on! Here’s an example: By emphasising Gladys’s daydreams of royal recognition, Harrison conveys how, for a family denied dignity and opportunity, the imagination becomes a vital refuge from an unjust reality—ultimately suggesting that dreams alone cannot deliver change, and that true escape demands the courage to speak and act. Now, you have two linked ideas: Dreams preserve dignity and hope for the characters in an unjust society. Dreaming alone, without action, cannot deliver genuine change. This provides a clear structure for an entire paragraph and sets you up to use a hinge sentence later to develop further layers—e.g., how this ’dreaming” can also reveal internalised colonial ideals or delay confrontation with injustice. By planning your paragraph in this way, you’ll ensure: Specific and sophisticated analysis Strong authorial intent Complex understanding of the characters’ motivations and Harrison’s critique of assimilationist policies in 1950s Australia
Analytical Text Response Structure

Topics There are three types of questions you may receive on a SAC or exam. These are the ’Discuss’, ’To what extent’ and ’Do you agree?’ statements. These are all tackled in a slightly varying manner. Discuss : This is probably the most common type of prompt that you will encounter. When dealing with such prompts, it is important that you discuss as many aspects of it as possible. One way to improve your discussion here is to include a paragraph that rebuts the prompt, like what you do in your persuasive essays. However, since it is a discuss prompt, you do not need to make the two sides of your argument extremely distinct. To what extent: These topics require you to make a decision. Never say ’it is to some extent’ in your contention. You really need to assign a portion of something. The best way to do this is to start with subordinating conjunctions like ’whilst” or ’although”. To What Extent questions rarely want you to agree or disagree - they want you to explore a shade of grey. How: The ’How’ topics usually focus on the construction of a text - your evidence should contain metalanguage like ’characterisation’ for instance.

Introduction The introduction is the first piece of your writing that will be examined. For this reason, it should be clear, direct and show your understanding of the topic. The introduction is there to show your examiner how you wish to respond to the set topic. If your topic contains a quote, ensure that you mention it somewhere in your introduction to show you are addressing the topic.
Structure An introduction must include the following:

Body Paragraphs The body paragraphs form majority of your essay. The body paragraphs are the main reasons that the contention your provided is true. Your body paragraphs need to be able to demonstrate your understanding of the text and the fluency of your language. You should ensure that every sentence (and thus every word) brings something towards your argument.

Structure Below is the structure for a body paragraph using ’Th.E. S.A.M.L.’ Method. This method of writing body paragraphs was developed in order to maximise possible marks. Only the ’TH’ and ’L’ elements of this structure need to be in the order given.

Basic Example ’Th.E S.A.M’ ’Obsession with one’s self is what ultimately leads to the downfall of characters. This can be seen with Norma exclaiming ‘who else?’ when Joe questions her about her script. The script itself is also a representation of Norma’s obsession as she has written several hundred pages where she stars in every scene. Wilder uses this to demonstrate that Norma is unable to move on from anything besides herself. The slow camera pan whenever the script is shown foreshadows Norma’s impending doom which links is inherently linked to her self-absorption. In this way, obsession with self ultimately leads to the downfall of several characters in Sunset Boulevard .’

t: Writing About Ideas: Adding Adjectives to Nouns
b: Writing About Ideas: Adding Adjectives to Nouns One of the easiest ways of improving your writing is to add adjectives to nouns, this helps us to create an ’idea phrase’. Here is a list of commonly used nouns in analytic writing, a good way is to use these as springboards to finding even more complex and advanced nouns. Idea Nouns for Analytical Writing Column 1 Column 2 Column 3 Column 4 Column 5 Growing up Survival Justice Friendship Loss Independence Conflict Humanity Family Guilt Coming of age Courage Prejudice Relationship Redemption Maturity Strength Oppression Belonging Hope Identity Bravery Injustice Loneliness Grief Discovery Persistence Power Loyalty Sorrow Revelation Fortitude Greed Betrayal Memory Concealment Heroism Selfishness Love Future Blossom Resilience Competence Hatred Past Deteriorate Cowardice Potential Envy Melancholy We then can pair these ideas with adjectives of degree - these tell us how big, small, frequent, fast or slow something is, here is a list to help build your writing vocabulary: small Adjectives of Degree for Analytical Writing Category BIG (Intensifying) SMALL (Minimising) SIZE Deep, Immense, Extensive, Overwhelming Empty, Insignificant, Little, Tiny STRENGTH Extreme, Powerful, Fierce, Determined Weak, Uncertain, Fragile, Feeble SPEED Rapid, Swift, Impulsive, Sudden Slow, Reluctant, Hesitant, Glacial CONSISTENCY Frequent, Constant, Incessant, Unchanging Unsteady, Unreliable, Erratic, Unstable

Hinge Sentences In your topic sentence, you can introduce two related ideas. This creates room for a more complex, insightful paragraph. But remember: when you shift focus from the first idea to the second, you must signal this change to the reader clearly. This is where the hinge sentence comes in. A hinge sentence acts like a bridge—it acknowledges what you’ve already explored, then transitions to the second idea you introduced in your topic sentence. Here’s how that works: By emphasising Gladys’s daydreams of royal recognition, Harrison conveys how, for a family denied dignity and opportunity, the imagination becomes a vital refuge from an unjust reality—ultimately suggesting that dreams alone cannot deliver change, and that true escape demands the courage to speak and act.

Evidence + Analysis of First Idea In the dream sequences where Gladys imagines being received by the Queen, Harrison frames fantasy not as idle escapism but as a shield of dignity for a woman excluded from the “decent” society she longs to join. Yet Nan Dear’s blunt dismissal—“And what bloody good would that do? Daydreams!” (p. 154)—exposes the limits of this refuge, highlighting how dreaming leaves the family’s material circumstances untouched.
Hinge Sentence (transitioning to Second idea) However, while Gladys’s dreams preserve her sense of self-worth, Harrison also reveals that fantasy alone cannot deliver her family from injustice.

Analysis of Second Idea Gladys’s transformation culminates in her address to the Rodney Shire Council, where she demands “proper schooling … The white people too — they need to be educated about us, and our ways” (p. 197). Harrison complicates the notion of escape by showing that it is voice and action, not fantasy, that begin to change the family’s circumstances, suggesting that dreams are only powerful when they inspire agency. By using a hinge sentence, you show your reader that you’re not just stacking ideas but developing an argument, deepening your interpretation, and showing the author’s nuanced message. Concluding Dependant Phrases Paired with Analytic Verbs Concluding Dependant Phrase Analytic Verb Even if Sophocles recognises... questions... Whilst Sophocles endorses... illustrates... Whether or not Sophocles implies... reveals... In order to explore ... Sophocles ... demonstrates...

Conclusion If you think of a conclusion as less of summarising everything, and more of combining all of these conflicting ideas and messages into one overall conclusive point. We can do this by using the following structure.

Structure The structure for a conclusion can follow the same structure as an introduction. However, instead of introducing the text, you are concluding your discussion. Here is an alternative method: Topic: ‘The dream sequences in Rainbow’s End are the characters’ only escape from the depressing reality of life.’ Do you agree? Conclusion Structure for Analytical Essay Writing Purpose in Conclusion Example Sentence Overview of how the whole text demonstrates the key concepts in the essay topic Fundamentally, Rainbow’s End explores the tension between dreams and reality, particularly how fantasy can offer dignity, but not deliverance, to those denied opportunity by an unjust society. Summary of the complications of this topic Throughout the play, Harrison complicates the notion of escape by showing that while Gladys’s daydreams preserve her sense of self-worth, they leave her family’s circumstances unchanged, challenging the romanticisation of hope without action. Focus on the key authorial message in conclusive language Therefore, Harrison critiques a society that forces its most marginalised to retreat into fantasy, asserting that genuine change lies not in dreaming of acceptance but in finding the voice to demand it, as Gladys does before the Shire Council.

Proofreading

Take a Break Before Proofreading After finishing your essay, your mind is still fresh with ideas, but it may be too familiar with the text to catch all mistakes. Step away from the essay for at least 10–15 minutes. This will help you approach it with fresh eyes and a clearer mindset when you return to review it.

Read Aloud Reading aloud forces you to slow down and focus on each word. It also helps you hear awkward phrasing or sentences that don’t flow well. Read your essay aloud, paying attention to how each sentence sounds. If something feels off or too long-winded, make adjustments.

Check for Structure and Coherence A strong essay is logically structured with clear arguments and smooth transitions. Ensure each paragraph has a clear topic sentence. Verify your argument flows logically from one point to the next. Ensure your introduction sets up your essay well and your conclusion summarises without introducing new ideas.

Look for Clarity and Precision Vague or unclear language can confuse your reader, even if the argument itself is solid. Check if any sentences can be more specific or concise. Remove unnecessary filler words like ’really,’ ’just,’ or ’quite.’ Replace vague words like ’things’ or ’stuff’ with more precise terms.

t: Verify Your Thesis and Topic Sentences
b: Verify Your Thesis and Topic Sentences Your thesis statement should be the central focus of your essay, and topic sentences should align with it. Double-check that: Your thesis is clear and present in the introduction. Each body paragraph clearly supports your thesis with an argument or evidence. Topic sentences match the content of their respective paragraphs.

Proofread for Grammar and Syntax Grammar mistakes, awkward phrasing, or incorrect sentence structure can weaken the professionalism of your essay. Read through your essay for common mistakes (e.g., subject-verb agreement, incorrect punctuation, run-on sentences). Use tools like Grammarly or even Microsoft Word’s spelling/grammar checker for a quick second look.

t: Check for Word Choice and Tone
b: Check for Word Choice and Tone The right word choice ensures your ideas are clear and impactful. Avoid overly complex words unless they are necessary for the context. Ensure you maintain an academic tone throughout the essay. Replace informal language (e.g., ’kids’ or ’stuff’) with more formal, specific terms.

Look for Repetition Repeating the same word or idea can weaken your essay’s overall impact. Identify words or phrases that appear too often. Use synonyms or restructure sentences to avoid redundancy. Consider varying sentence structures to keep the reader engaged. Insight’s Proofreading Checklist

Check Punctuation and Spacing Simple punctuation errors or inconsistent spacing can distract the reader. Verify commas, full stops, and apostrophes are used correctly. Ensure there is proper spacing after full stops and between paragraphs.

t: Ask Someone Else to Read It
b: Ask Someone Else to Read It Fresh eyes can often spot mistakes you may have missed. Ask a peer, teacher, or family member to read your essay. Be open to their feedback and use it to improve your writing.

t: Ensure It Meets the Task Requirements
b: Ensure It Meets the Task Requirements Your essay should always answer the question and meet the criteria specified in the prompt. Revisit the question to ensure your essay directly addresses it. Check that all key parts of the task are covered and that your argument is relevant.

Personal Journeys From the Study Design Explorations of ’life’ or biographical explorations - telling our stories, telling others’ stories, the problem of telling stories, appropriation of stories, who tells the stories and our history, missing stories, marginalised and elevated stories. Students could explore personal milestones, the effects of key events on their lives, or explore these ideas through the eyes of others. Students who have migrated can explore their stories of movement and disruption. They can explore the expectations of change and the language of a new place and culture. Insight’s Different Forms of Text

Writing Writing about personal journeys explores both the physical world and the internal world of oneself and others. Reading and crafting texts about journeys provide opportunities to consider new places, perspectives, and self-discovery. Personal journeys involve various experiences, including challenges, growth, and self-discovery. Writing about personal journeys allows individuals to share unique experiences with others. REMINDER: You have access to the exam generator and the prompt generator to practice your Section B skills.

Ideas A personal journey can involve: Travel to a physical place. Psychological or emotional growth and change. A combination of both, where physical travel leads to new insights. Interior journeys may take a long time to occur due to significant events. The most important aspect of a personal journey is change – the individual or group is fundamentally altered by it. 2024 VCAA English Exam - Section B for Personal Journeys

Essay Structure
Introduction

t: What you are trying to achieve
b: What you are trying to achieve The purpose of an introduction is to ’introduce’ both the irrefutable facts about the text you will be analysing, and your interpretation of the text. The best way to do this is to connect the text’s plain details with your early analysis of what the contention, tone and intended audience.
Include Context and contention Details (writer, publication, date and text type) Major persuasive devices Tone (consistant or shifting) Intended audience and how you know this

Revise A common mistake students make is that they turn their introductions into lists of everything they need to include. Remember to express yourself clearly, professionally and with style. This is the introduction to your analysiss and sets your assesor’s expectations for the rest of the piece.

Sentence structure The first part of your introduction should focus on what issue is being discussed and why it is important. This will help you to create an opening to demonstrate a strong understanding of the article, for example: The current trend of dangerous Tik-Tok challenges has once again sparked debate over whether we should be restricting access to the wildly popular app. If we break it down it becomes: The current trend - Phrase of recency of dangerous Tik-tok challenges - Incident has once again sparked - Verb phrase debate - Issue Here are some sample sentence builders: Framing Contemporary Issues in Writing: Phrases of Recency, Cause, and Contention Phrases of Recency Verb Phrases Issues The latest... ...has once again raised... issue The present... ...has sparked... debate The recent... ...has led to... argument The earlier... ...has motivated... discussions The current... ...has caused... disputes Once again... ...has generated... altercation Recently... ...has engendered... discourse Lately... ...has provoked... consultation Over the past year... ...has propagated... dialogue

Key Information Phrase In this section we should include key information that is usually found in the background information section of the text, including: Author’s name Connection of author to text The form of the piece, the date and where it was published How this links back to the issue you identified in your first sentence For example, this sentence can look like: Dr Marko Shellius, CEO of Psychiatric Safe Australia, has written a submission to the parliamentary inquiry into social media, in particular addressing Tik-Tok’s burgeoning influence over Australian teens. If we break it down, it looks like this: Authorial Context and Publication Framing for Argument Analysis Author Connection of Author to the Issue Form and Publisher Link Back to the Issue Dr Marko Shellius CEO of Psychiatric Safe Australia Has written a submission to the parliamentary inquiry on social media In particular addressing TikTok’s burgeoning influence over Australian teens This order can also be moved around as well: Form, Author, and Contextual Framing of the Issue Form and Publisher Author Connection of Author to the Issue Link Back to the Issue In a written submission to the parliamentary inquiry into social media, Dr Marko Shellius CEO of Psychiatric Safe Australia Addresses TikTok’s burgeoning influence over Australian teens Here are some sentence starters to help build these sentences: Author Connection, Form of Publication, and Issue Framing Phrases Connection of Author to Issue Form and Loc

Tones Analysing argument involves understanding how authors use language to persuade their audience. This includes identifying the author’s tone, which refers to the overall feeling or attitude conveyed in the text. Recognising shifts in tone is crucial as it often accompanies changes in argument strategies. Authors use tone to appeal to the reader’s emotions, establish credibility, and guide the reader’s interpretation of the argument. This is the perfect place for you to show the breadth of your vocabulary. Choose a specific word that relates to the article. Always avoid using the terms ’Positive’, ’Negative’ and ’Neutral’.” Positive Tone Vocabulary Bank Amazed Calm Appreciative Affectionate Amiable Astonished Casual Approving Benevolent Amused Attentive Collected Assuring Compassionate Cheerful Curious Composed Confident Concerned Delighted Eager Content Determined Considerate Ecstatic Interested Peaceful Encouraging Consoling Elated Keen Pleasant Grateful Empathetic Energetic Polite Relaxed Hopeful Emphatic Enthusiastic Startled Relieved Inspiring Friendly Excited Stunned Serene Optimistic Loving Exuberant Surprised Pleased Merciful Happy Promising Romantic Humourous Proud Soothing Jovial Respectful Supportive Joyful Reverent Sympathetic Jubilant Sanguine Thoughtful Playful Satisfied Vibrant Thankful Vivacious Neutral Tone Vocabulary Bank Admonitory Factual Patriotic Allusive Formal Personal Authoritative Frank Picturesque Balance Honest Questioning Blunt Informal Reflect

Audience The audience refers to anyone who is viewing, reading or listening to a text. When writing an analysing argument essay, it isn’t enough to just say your audience is ‘everyone’ or ‘all Australians’. You need to refine your idea of who you are writing for! Three questions to ask yourself What is the author writing about? Who would be interested in this topic? Is there more than one audience who might be interested in this? Refining Audience The key take-away is that you need to be as specific as possible when stating the intended audience.
Body Paragraphs

t: What you are trying to achieve
b: What you are trying to achieve Each body paragraph should explore, in sequence, the major arguments that the author uses to support their contention. Remember you are being assessed on your ability to analyse the arguments the author uses and how the language they have chosen supports these.

Step 1: What? Outline what the author is arguing in your own words. Make a comment on structure; if this is the first or final argument then what is the reason the author introduced this at this time.

Step 2: How? Begin by identifying the persuasive language features that the author uses to substantiate their argument, include a quote illustrating your point (but no more than 5 words). But this is not enough, then you need to analyse the effectiveness of this approach and discuss how it positions the audience. If there is an image associated with this argument, you should talk about it here - treat it like a persuasive device. Depending on the argument, you may want to talk about how a group of persuasive devices work together. Note: Try to avoid repetition by constantly stating ’The author uses X which makes the audience feel Y. This can come across tacky and quite robotic. To see examples of how this can be done in a successful way, look at the three high-scoring essays below.

Step 3: Why? Finally, you need to infer why the author chose this approach, what is their persuasive intent? How would it appeal to their particular audience? How does it impact the tone of the article? How does this support their contention?
Step 4: Examine (Optional) Does the tone change for this argument? Does the structure change? How does this position the audience?
Conclusion

t: What you are trying to achieve
b: What you are trying to achieve In your conclusion, you are exploring both how the text concludes and wrapping up your analysis at the same time. It is an opportunity to discuss the text as a whole, and emphasises your analytical skills.

Include Comment on the structure of the article, including whether there is a consistent or changing tone of writing. Focus on the last paragraph of the article, evaluating how it supports the writer’s contention. Evaluate whether the article developed more trust in the readers and explain why.

Note Some students find they don’t have time for a conclusion. This isn’t ideal, but it isn’t the end of the world, as most of this information should have been explicit throughout. If you are running short on time, a sentence summarising how each argument leads to the contention is sufficient.

Complex Sentence and Paragraph Building When analysing persuasive texts, it’s not enough to simply spot techniques—you need to explain what is happening, why it’s there, and how it affects the audience. Students often start with short, seperate sentences: ’The writer uses emotive language. This makes the audience feel sympathy’ A stronger version combines ideas into one complex sentence: ‘Describing the victims as “cruelly abandoned” evokes a deep sense of sympathy in the audience, prompting them to see the government as neglectful and morally failing in its duty of care.’

Techniques for Stronger Sentences Subordinating conjunctions – because, although, while, since, if Embedded quotes – weave quotes into the sentence naturally Relative clauses – who, which, that Appositives – a technique aimed at… By applying these sentence-building tools, your analysis will become more precise, connected, and persuasive—mirroring the sophistication expected in high-level responses. To help you identify and discuss these elements in a clear, structured way, we will now look at a step-by-step framework that breaks persuasive writing into its key components.

W.A.S.T.E.: F.A.T.! This acronym is designed to assist you in crafting complex and sophisticated sentences that strengthen your analytical writing. In higher-scoring essays, writers deliberately weave these elements into their work to add depth, clarity, and precision. While it is not necessary to include every element in every individual sentence, you should aim to integrate all of them across the body of your essay. This means that, by the end of each body paragraph, the reader should be able to identify how you have employed these elements to develop your argument, enhance your expression, and engage the audience effectively. Consistent and purposeful use of these techniques will not only improve sentence complexity but also demonstrate a mature and well-structured writing style. W ords – Focus on the most persuasive words or phrases of the piece. A rgument – What is the supporting argument of each section? S tructure – Where is this located in the piece and why? T one – How does the author apply tone to be more persuasive in each section? E ffect upon the audience – How does it position the audience to: F eel? A ct? T hink?

W.A.S.T.E.: F.A.T. Medium Example The editorial uses emotionally charged words such as “climate catastrophe”, “tipping point”, and “irreversible damage” to stress the seriousness of the issue. The supporting argument is that urgent government action is necessary to prevent catastrophic environmental collapse. These phrases appear in the introduction so that the reader is immediately confronted with the scale of the problem. The author adopts an alarmist and urgent tone to heighten concern and prompt immediate attention. This positions the audience to feel fear and anxiety about the future, to act by pressuring politicians or signing petitions, and to think that if society does not act now, it will be too late.

W.A.S.T.E.: F.A.T. High Example The editorial uses emotive phrases such as “climate catastrophe”, “tipping point”, and “irreversible damage” in its introduction to immediately confront the reader with the scale of the problem. By presenting the argument that urgent government action is needed to prevent environmental collapse, the writer establishes the issue as both pressing and unavoidable. Through an alarmist and urgent tone, the piece positions the audience to feel fear, act by pressuring politicians or signing petitions, and think that immediate intervention is essential.

Analysing Written Language
Terms and Strategies

Specific Word Choice (word connotation) Writers choose particular words to provoke an emotional reaction in the reader or emphasise an idea. For example, if writing about abortion, the author may choose; "I read with disgust that the government is to ... kill, slay, slaughter, murder, butcher, exterminate, destroy, sacrifice, execute ... Emotive language is language that is not logical or rational - it reflects and expresses an emotional or subjective reaction. Such words help create mood. Many words have connotations or associations that the writer uses with deliberate intent to create a desired effect in the reader. Words may have positive or negative connotations. They may evoke intense imagery. You will be expected to discuss some targeted words and their relative connotations in your language analysis piece.

Particular language structure and features Inclusive language (we, our) may be used to give the audience a sense of being part of a wider community of thought/action. Modifiers and qualifiers are words used to denote a level of impact. There is a difference between a writer choosing to use ’some’ instead of ’all’ or ’most’ instead of ’many’. Phrases such as ’very good’ or ’basically good’ also suggest different meaning to the reader. Students analysing language need to consider why specific lexical strings have been chosen by the writer as a form of persuasion.

Euphemisms Euphemisms are words sometimes used to soften a more accurate but seemingly more offensive word or phrase. For example, euphemisms for ’die’ include; passed away, lose one’s life, kick the bucket, croak. A company may say they are ’downsizing’ instead offiring workers from the job.

Hypothetical Scenarios Hypothetical scenarios are used to prompt the reader to imagine possible outcomes, often beginning with phrases like “Imagine if...” . These speculative examples draw attention to the consequences of actions or inactions, even if they are not real or likely. Writers use this technique to provoke reflection and emotional investment from the reader. For example: “Imagine if the world was ruled by tyrants who dominated our every thought daily...”

Repetition Repetition involves deliberately repeating words or phrases for emphasis and to make an idea more memorable. Anaphora: repetition at the start of successive clauses. e.g. School is distracting. School is boring. School sucks. Epistrophe: repetition at the end of clauses. e.g. Follow the rules. Obey the rules. These are the rules.

Puns Puns are humorous wordplays based on similar sounds or multiple meanings. They are often used to lighten tone or ridicule opposing views. Some puns also carry double meanings, especially in persuasive or satirical writing. e.g. “Time flies like an arrow; fruit flies like a banana.”

Irony Irony occurs when the intended meaning of a statement contrasts with its literal meaning. Writers often use irony to highlight absurdities or criticise ideas indirectly. e.g. “Lovely day,” said during a storm.

Sarcasm Sarcasm is a biting, mocking form of expression usually intended to hurt, ridicule, or convey contempt. It often overlaps with irony but has a more cutting tone. e.g. “Nice face. Shame about the rest.”

Exaggeration / Hyperbole This technique involves overstating a point for dramatic effect. It can be used to emphasise importance or provoke an emotional response. e.g. “I’d rather cut off my arm than go back to work under those conditions!”

Humour Humour engages the reader by entertaining them. It can also make controversial ideas more palatable or draw attention to flaws in opposing arguments. It is often used as a non-threatening way to critique.

Satire Satire combines humour and criticism to ridicule individuals, institutions, or ideas. It often exposes hypocrisy or absurdity, making serious points in a humorous way. e.g. A TV show like Frontline that uses mock-journalism to critique the media.
Oxymoron An oxymoron combines two contradictory terms to highlight contrast or provoke thought. It is often used to emphasise complexity or irony. e.g. deafening silence, wise fool, bitter sweet
Assonance Assonance is the repetition of vowel sounds within words or phrases to create rhythm, mood, or emphasis. Often used poetically or in slogans. e.g. “cool, calm and collected”

Simile A simile compares one thing with another using the words ‘like’ or ‘as’ . It helps readers understand unfamiliar concepts by likening them to familiar ones, often to create vivid imagery. e.g. My bedroom is like a pigsty. She is as skinny as a rake.

Metaphor A metaphor is an implied comparison that states one thing is another, thereby transferring qualities from one to the other. Metaphors can make writing more persuasive by enhancing imagery and emotional appeal. e.g. I stepped into my pigsty, shut the door and climbed into bed. He is a pillar of strength.

Rhetorical Questions These are questions posed for effect rather than to elicit an answer. They imply that the answer is self-evident, guiding the audience to agree with the writer’s point. e.g. Do you want to see your wife and children out on the streets in rags? Of course you don’t!

Scapegoats A scapegoat is a person or group blamed for something they are not truly responsible for. This technique simplifies complex issues and focuses the reader’s anger on a convenient target. e.g. All our crime and unemployment problems are because of the government.

Assumptions Assumptions involve treating beliefs or opinions as facts without proof. These are often subtle and can influence readers by appealing to what is generally believed to be true. e.g. He must be smart because he is on the computer every day.
Presumptions Presumptions occur when statements are accepted as truths without critical examination. They appeal to common beliefs or cultural norms. e.g. If it’s natural, it’s good for you.

Analogy An analogy compares two things that are alike in some respects to clarify a point or persuade readers that what applies to one must apply to the other. e.g. If birds can fly because they have wings, then maybe if we develop mechanical wings we can fly too. e.g. The human brain is like a computer.

Expert Opinion This technique involves citing someone perceived to have authority or expertise on a topic. It lends credibility to an argument and can be persuasive even if the “expert” is unnamed. e.g. In the thirty-five years I was Head Surgeon at...

Case Studies and Anecdotes Case studies and anecdotes are used to personalise or humanise an argument. Anecdotes are usually emotive and personal; case studies more formal and factual. Both aim to build credibility and emotional appeal. e.g. Pool fencing should be mandatory because my neighbour’s child drowned in an unfenced pool.

Empty Truths Also known as tautologies, these are statements that repeat the same idea unnecessarily, often sounding meaningful but lacking substance. e.g. Boys will be boys. Enough is enough. Business is business.

Cause and Effect This technique suggests that one event directly causes another. It is persuasive because it offers logical explanation, though causality may not always be valid. e.g. Peter crashed his car. Peter died.

t: Ends and Means / Means to an End
b: Ends and Means / Means to an End This approach justifies an action based on the outcome, even if the action itself is questionable. e.g. A criminal donates to a playground; the donation is good, but the source of funds is illegal.

Consequence of a Fact This presents a pragmatic response to an existing situation by arguing that something should be done simply because a condition exists. e.g. Nuclear power exists, so we might as well use it.

t: Judging an Action by its Consequences
b: Judging an Action by its Consequences This technique justifies or condemns an action based on its results. It focuses on outcomes rather than intent. e.g. Stealing from the rich to give to the poor is acceptable because the poor are hungry.

Sweeping Statements / Generalisations These make broad claims that apply to a group without considering individual differences. Generalisations can sound convincing but often oversimplify. e.g. All teenagers are irresponsible.

Cliché Clichés are overused phrases that offer familiar shortcuts to meaning. Though persuasive due to their recognisability, they can also weaken writing if not used purposefully. e.g. What goes around comes around.

Co-existence This assumes an automatic relationship between ideas or traits, such as assuming someone is good simply because they attend church. e.g. She goes to the local church, so she must be a good person.

Guilt by Association This technique links people, ideas or groups with undesirable individuals or arguments in order to discredit them. e.g. Premier Kennett has behaved like Hitler since coming to power.

Questioning Motive This involves undermining an argument by questioning the motives of the speaker, diverting attention away from the argument itself. e.g. The Health Minister’s decision to close the hospital is just an excuse to build a monument to himself.

Figures and Statistics Numbers and data are used to give the illusion of objectivity and reliability. However, the reader must critically assess source, sample size, relevance, and interpretation. e.g. 300 people died on our roads last year...

Discredit Another Argument This technique aims to dismantle opposing viewpoints by exposing flaws or inconsistencies in them. It draws attention away from the original argument and weakens the opposing side’s credibility.

Attack This technique involves criticising or mocking opposing arguments or individuals. It is designed to weaken the opposition’s credibility and make their position less persuasive. Writers may use name-calling, ridicule, or other personal attacks to influence the audience’s perception. One form of attack is labelling the opposition in an attempt to belittle them.

Flattery and Praise This technique involves listing individuals or groups who agree with the writer’s position in a positive light. By using terms that convey admiration or respect, the writer bolsters the authority of their own argument. e.g. “Leading scientists support this research.”

t: Use of Punctuation and Printing Style
b: Use of Punctuation and Printing Style Writers use punctuation and formatting deliberately to add emphasis, create subtext, or manipulate tone and flow. Common strategies include: Inverted commas – suggest doubt or irony, even when quoting. Exclamation marks – show emotional emphasis or urgency. Dashes – indicate pauses, hesitation or parenthetical ideas. Long sentences – may signal sophistication or rambling. Short sentences – create punch, pace and immediacy. Italics, CAPITALISATION, bold , underlining – all used for emphasis.

Vocabulary Use The choice of vocabulary significantly affects tone and credibility. Writers use sophisticated or precise word choice to sound more authoritative or to target specific reader emotions. Students should be aware of: Emotive connotations (e.g. slaughter vs kill ) Word strength and subtlety (e.g. sporadic vs occasional ) Replacing vague or weak terms with more accurate or academic language. Improving vocabulary can enhance clarity and persuasiveness. For example: Replace “straight away” with “immediately” Replace “in the next paragraph” with “in the following section” Replace “always” with “consistently”

Analysing Visual Language Many students find the analysis of images an accessible and enjoyable part of Year 12 English. It is important, however, to prepare for a variety of visual elements that may appear on your Unit 3 SAC task on analysing argument, as well as on Section C of the end-of-year examination. Below are some elements to keep an eye out for.

Context and Position In order to fully understand the meaning of the visual elements in a text, you need to understand the context. Be sure to read any background information accompanying the task so that you know what the main issue is. Both the background information and the written material will help give you a sense of the visual’s purpose. Remember that the focus of Area of Study 2 in the Unit 3 SAC and in Section C of the exam is on the analysis of argument. Noticing where and why a visual element is placed in the written piece can help you to tackle the idea of how the argument is structured. Is the visual at the start of the article to elicit empathy or shock? Is it in the middle to reinforce a particular piece of evidence or reason? Is it at the end to consolidate the argument as a whole? Is it a frame or border to the written piece that contributes to the overall approach and tone? Alternatively, if the visual is a completely separate text from the written piece, does it present the same or a different point of view on the issue? Ask yourself: where is the visual positioned and why does it matter?

Colour and Design Elements Although the task material for Section C of the exam will be printed in black and white, your Unit 3 SAC might well contain colour images. If this is the case, you can certainly explore the mood that is created by the use of colour, as well as any relevant connotations of the colour scheme. In a black-and-white image it is possible to discuss degrees of shading and how these affect meaning. It is important to remember that visual language can pertain to design elements in the text you are analysing. For example, in the task material for Section C of the VCAA sample exam the speaker’s first slide has illustrations incorporated into the year ‘2010’. The embedded images of fish, a flamingo, waves, a tree, and an adult and child holding hands help to promote the speaker’s arguments about the beauty of biodiversity and humanity’s central involvement in its protection. Also be alert to the design elements of internet text types such as web pages and blogs. You can, for example, discuss the use of icons, how they position the viewer and the symbolism embedded in them. Ask yourself: how do design elements, colour and/or shading of the visual contribute to its meaning? Insight Example Image Analysis

Cartoons Cartoons often accompany an opinion piece; they can also be stand-alone texts offering a viewpoint on a current issue – especially one that involves politicians or high-profile figures. To understand a cartoon it can be important to have an understanding of the issues circulating in the media in any given week. As I write, there are two quite different cartoons in The Age and The Australian that contain an image of a whiteboard, among other elements. To make sense of both cartoons, a reader would need to know that earlier in the week security guards in Parliament House used a whiteboard to shield a politician from photographers and journalists. Without this knowledge it would be difficult, if not impossible, to interpret the cartoons. Try to stay informed about current affairs as you practise analysing media texts, so that you can grasp their full meanings. Cartoons tend to exaggerate the features of those they represent and use minimal – often quite pithy – text. Be mindful of where this text is: does it appear in a speech bubble or balloon and therefore represent a person’s thoughts or speech? Or is the text elsewhere – on a sign, say, or framed as a caption – providing additional context or comment? The tone of a cartoon is an important feature to note. Political cartoons in particular are often sarcastic and position the reader to be critical of politicians and/or their views and policies. Ask yourself: how does the cartoon make me feel? Your response will give y

Photographs Photographs can provide a wealth of material for analysis. Look for a range of elements, including: whether the shot is low-angle (looking up at the subject), high-angle (looking down on the subject) or straight-on – for example, a low-angle shot of a child might give the impression that they have been able to overcome adversity and achieve success, whereas a high-angle shot might suggest that a person is vulnerable or remorseful whether the subject is in the foreground (making them very prominent) or background (emphasising their context) whether elements of the photograph are blurred or in soft focus whether a filter (e.g. warm or cool) is used where the horizon is placed in a landscape shot (e.g. far away can signal isolation or opportunity) what is actually in the frame (mise en scène) and what might have been left out whether a text overlay has been used and how that frames your reading of the photograph. Ask yourself: What would the impact be if I changed an element of the photograph (e.g. camera angle or focus)? Answering this question will give you better insight into what the photograph is intending to achieve.

Charts and Diagrams Charts and diagrams containing numerical information can be used to present evidence. Although you are not in a position to question the data, you can explore how the information and its visual presentation are used to strengthen and develop an argument. It is good to watch for: what the visual elements suggest and how they connect to each other whether a background image can be seen beneath a written overlay (e.g. an image of a gun beneath statistics about the number of deaths caused by shooting) whether data is attributed to an authority (such as the Australian Bureau of Statistics). Sometimes charts and diagrams can look dry and intimidating. Ask yourself: how does the information relate to the written text or texts? Does it support or contradict a written text and, if so, how?

Concluding Remarks For the Exam, it is imperative that you compare the written and visual material supplied. It is also wise to compare the written and visual texts in the examination. Comparing written and visual material in terms of the arguments they present and the ways in which they position the audience will give your analysis more depth and cohesion, and demonstrate a stronger understanding of each. t: This Book Week, let’s celebrate the power of reading (not just the costumes)
b: This Book Week, let’s celebrate the power of reading (not just the costumes) By Tracey Cheung — August 21, 2023, 11.25am It’s officially that time of year when parents scour the shops for the perfect Book Week costume. For some, making sure their little one looks just like their chosen storybook character involves face-painting, finding the right glasses, hat, bow or cape — sometimes tail and ears. Among all this, it can be easy to lose the true meaning behind Book Week. Wendy Rapee, chair of Children’s Book Council of Australia, says the event is an opportunity to draw attention to the joy of reading and for advocating for stories in children’s lives. Rapee explains this is the reason behind this year’s CBCA Children’s Book Week theme: Read, Inspire, Grow . “Like dressing up, reading books presents the opportunity to walk in someone else’s shoes, to experience adventures, feel things they have never felt and hopefully learn something about the world they live in,” says Rapee. Over the years, I have found that reading stories to my daughter has helped me teach her helpful life skills and ways of thinking. And I’m not the only one with this experience. Sue Cahill, a student wellbeing leader at St Charles Borromeo Primary School in Melbourne, says she finds storybooks extremely powerful in teaching her school children how to deal with various issues, such as school refusal, friendships and bullying. She used the book I Am Jack by Susanne Gervay to help two boys in a bullying si

Learning valuable relationship skills Venita Dimos is a lawyer, mediator and the author of children’s book series Mini and Milo . Each book in the series has a different theme, including mindset, communication and listening. As a lawyer and mediator, Dimos has witnessed the profound impact of unresolved conflict in families and workplaces. Her series was driven by a heartfelt need to teach children key relationship skills early on in life. “Managing conflict is not a skill you are born with – it’s a skill you need to learn,” she says. “Unfortunately, most people don’t learn this in early childhood, so grow up learning on the job with their relationships at stake.” Recently, I read Dimos’ Mini and Milo: The Teeny-Tiny Voice . At the time, my daughter had picked up a swear word from somewhere. When I told her to stop saying it, she said to me, “I want to stop but the voice inside my head keeps telling me to say it.” The book taught her that we have greater power over our thoughts than we think, which eventually helped her to stop swearing.

Discussion of sensitive topics Books have also helped encourage conversations with my daughter about issues I have otherwise felt unsure about how to open myself. For example, while I found it awkward to start conversations about the topic of body safety, reading My Body! What I Say Goes! by Jayneen Sanders helped break the ice. Lucy Kippist, 44, a community manager of Mums & Co and mother of two from Sydney, found books helpful when helping her children get through the grief of losing their grandparents. She says How Can I Help Papa? A Child’s Journey Through Loss and Healing by Elissa Al-Chokhachy helped give her children new words to describe their feelings around grief. While reading, they learned that part of grieving someone you loved is to remember the happy times. “It elicited some happy memories with their grandfather. We talked about the more fun things, like how he used to do Lego with them,” says Kippist. Alyson Simpson, a professor of English and literacy education at the University of Sydney, says parents don’t need to expect that a reading session will be a literacy lesson. “Allow children to explore the books,” she says. “My grandson finds more things in the pictures than I do sometimes.”

Fostering friendships Elisha Casagrande, 35, found that her son was able to find a message in a book without any prompts from her. The Brisbane-based health coach recently started home-schooling her son, who was having trouble being apart from his friends, so she decided to read The Giving Tree by Shel Silverstein to him. In the book, a boy makes brief visits to a ’living” tree several times over the years, until he becomes an old man. “My son picked up that relationships can still last, provided there’s some connection over time, like the man who kept connecting with that tree,” says Casagrande. When I asked my 5-year-old daughter who she was going to dress up as for her first Book Week, it was an easy choice: Cinderella. Although I know she is drawn to the story because of the pretty princess, after reading the book together, I also know she understands the morals behind the story, such as the importance of kindness.

t: I’m Melbourne Grammar’s school captain and I’m gay. The Presbyterian Church would have me sacked
b: I’m Melbourne Grammar’s school captain and I’m gay. The Presbyterian Church would have me sacked By Daniel Cash — Melbourne Grammar School Captain April 18, 2023 — 7.30pm The Presbyterian Church of Australia has declared its right to refuse school leadership opportunities to students based on sexuality or premarital sexual activity. This comes in response to the Australian Law Reform Commission’s review of discrimination laws. The Church has reasoned that, in the case of discriminating against students based on sexual preference, gay students “would not be able to give appropriate Christian leadership in a Christian school which requires modelling Christian living.” I write this as captain of Melbourne Grammar School. I write this as someone who is gay, a fact about myself which I cannot change. I also write this as someone confused — surely ’modelling Christian living” is not well exemplified in the practice of exclusion or discrimination? The Christian faith is built on the example Christ set in his life. His revolutionary compassion, so radical then as it still is now — care for the outcasts, the lepers, the unclean — inform the values of Christianity and direct the moral constitution of the Church. Jesus shocked the established powers of his time, his love the instrument of a legacy which left a world faith developing in its wake. His example leads me to wonder what Christ would make of this situation. If he was confronted with a body of young people — all, to remind the

t: Confession of a Knox Grammar old boy: my son won’t lose sleep as I have
b: Confession of a Knox Grammar old boy: my son won’t lose sleep as I have By Malcolm Knox — Journalist, author and columnist September 2, 2022 — 10.15am An old schoolmate invited me to his home for dinner. We hadn’t seen each other in the 30 years since we had left Knox Grammar, the school that hit the headlines this week after boys were suspended or left after posting what their headmaster, Scott James, called “inappropriate images and … offensive commentary in an online private chatroom”. Much of the content was so stomach-turning it could not be published by The Daily Telegraph , which broke the story. Back to my schoolmate. Towards the end of a pleasant evening, he revealed he had never got over his bitterness at the horrendous abuse he had received at Knox (from other students, not the teachers who were secretly abusing boys sexually in that same era). The damage he revealed, even as an adult with a good life deriving from his mighty intelligence, his lovely young family and the educational headstart he’d had in life, was chilling. I thought: Uh-oh. After a silence, I said, “I want to know – was I responsible for any of that?” He said with a sharpness that showed how hurt he still was, “I wouldn’t have you in my home if you were one of them.” The awful truth for me was, I honestly wouldn’t have known if I had or hadn’t been “one of them”. t: Don’t ask me to give the Queen a minute’s silence, ask me for the truth about British colonialism
b: Don’t ask me to give the Queen a minute’s silence, ask me for the truth about British colonialism By Lidia Thorpe Wednesday, 14 September 2022 — 12.07 AEST ’We’re not grieving a singular human life, we’re reeling from the violence that is the legacy of the monarchy.” — Photograph: Mick Tsikas/AAP News of the Queen’s death broke on the same day as my cousin’s funeral. My cousin, like more than 500 First Nations people in the last 30 years alone, died in custody. I was notified on Monday of another death in custody. The institutions that British colonisation brought here — from the education that erases us to the prisons that kill us — are designed to destroy the oldest living culture in the world. That’s the legacy of the crown in this country. The “British empire” declared a war on these shores, against this country’s First Nations peoples. This led to massacres. And you want a minute’s silence from me? Their war continues and is still felt today — on our children, our men, our land, our water, the air we breathe. Yet we’re meant to kneel to the colonising force with our hands on our hearts? It is insulting that the prime minister, Anthony Albanese, has called the 22 September public holiday a “National Day of Mourning for Her Majesty The Queen” , when First Nations people have called for 26 January to be acknowledged as a Day of Mourning since 1938. We called for a Day of Mourning so that this country could understand how we’re still affected by colonisation today. We’re not

t: Strapleton Community Voice: Public Post by Daphne Lower
b: Strapleton Community Voice: Public Post by Daphne Lower Background information The website Strapleton Community Voice has a social media page for the exclusive use of residents of the town of Strapleton. Members use Strapleton Community Voice to share information and concerns about the local area. Daphne Lower is a member of the group and created the post on the following page. Strapleton Community Voice Public group 11,727 followers Daphne Lower 2 October at 11:35 I want to share with you a dilemma I encountered this morning as I was taking my usual stroll around our picturesque park and lovely lake area. I noticed a new sign near the water saying: DO NOT FEED THE BIRDS. Metres away from the new sign stood a man and two children busily feeding a flock of wild ducks, totally ignoring the very clear message not to do so. What should I do, if anything? This is what went through my mind … When I was growing up, we often fed wild birds. My mum had a little wooden bird feeder hanging from a tree in our front yard. Every morning, she’d lower the rope attached to the bird feeder, place a sprinkling of seed inside and carefully hoist it back up. That bird feeder attracted all sorts of beloved parrots and we would watch them, in awe of their majesty. On family trips to the beach, we’d eat hot chips and throw some to the swarm of seagulls begging at our feet. Even on the few times we ventured overseas, we saw lots of people donating scraps of their lunch to pigeons or scattering bread

t: Our Local Gym: Emergency Meeting Speech by Sandy Gestalt
b: Our Local Gym: Emergency Meeting Speech by Sandy Gestalt Background information Our Local Gym is a fitness centre that caters to a local community. Until recently, the facility was supported by a council subsidy. This funding has been withdrawn and reallocated to other council projects. The Our Local Gym committee has called an emergency meeting for members of the gym to vote on a proposal to keep the facilities operating. The chair of the Our Local Gym committee, Sandy Gestalt, presented the following speech with accompanying slides. Thank you for attending this emergency meeting to vote on the future of Our Local Gym. We find ourselves in an uncomfortable position, but as a group of people who have worked together – worked out together – for years, we know that together we can save Our Local Gym, and ensure its future. Before we move on to possible solutions to our situation, I want to acknowledge the history and the value of Our Local Gym to both you, our members, and to the wider community. Our Local Gym has been in operation for 47 years. We built it from scratch with council support, local fundraisers and fees from you, our members. We have, for years, been a safe space for all people, and for the young and young-at-heart. This space is where you have built your fitness, your strength and your confidence. While larger commercial gyms have flourished, they cater for a different clientele – for people who perhaps are looking for a more competitive place to achieve their f

t: Lighting the way: a new year, a new celeberation
b: Lighting the way: a new year, a new celeberation by Jack Adult, 2025 Background information Every year the regional town of Timberoona hosts a fireworks display to usher in the new year. This annual tradition has been an important event on the community calendar for more than 50 years. Over the past few years, however, some residents have been concerned about the impact of the event on the town and have been seeking alternative ways to celebrate New Year’s Eve. Local resident JackAdut submitted the following article with accompanying images to the community newsletter Timberoona: Talk of the town. Like most people in our community, I consider New Year’s Eve to be a highlight on my social calendar. I look forward to participating in the many festivities held in the beautiful setting of Timberoona Park. The family picnics, roaming entertainment, food vans and showcasing of local musical talent in the rotunda create the perfect backdrop for the main event: the annual fireworks display. The spectacular pyrotechnics can be viewed from vantage points across the park and beyond, with veterans of the event knowing exactly where to set up their picnic rugs and folding chairs to secure the best view. It is with great anticipation that I await the collective countdown to midnight and the first big bang that reverberates through the air! As you can see, I’m all for the opportunity to welcome the new year with a big celebration, but it’s time to re-think the use of fireworks. It wasn’t un

t: An Open Letter from the Student Music Leadership Group, Narrow Valley
b: An Open Letter from the Student Music Leadership Group, Narrow Valley Background information The Narrow Valley region comprises small rural towns that border a large regional city. A group of young musicians who live in Narrow Valley have decided they want more opportunities to perform in their area. The group has written an open letter to the Narrow Valley community, which has been published on social media and in the local newspaper. In addition, copies have been displayed in shops and businesses. The following text is this open letter with images added by the young musicians. An open letter to the Narrow Valley community, young and old, who wish to see our region rejuvenated … Please allow us to introduce ourselves! We are a growing group of high-school music students, teachers and mentors from the five Narrow Valley secondary colleges. We are eager to seize opportunities to bring dynamic and long-overdue changes to our region through an inclusive, youth-driven, energetic and shiny new music festival. Our festival will provide a space for all budding musicians, regardless of age and abilities, tastes and styles to come together and enjoy music. Our passion, talents and all-encompassing abilities enable us to experiment with, explore and master all types of music, including rock, pop, opera, hip-hop (to name just a few), which are all played by our various orchestras, ensembles and solo artists. t: Hapsey Happenings – Transcript of podcast, episode 32
b: Hapsey Happenings – Transcript of podcast, episode 32 Background information Hapsey Happenings is a podcast hosted by lifelong Hapsey resident Janelle Tanley. She discusses local events and issues, and promotes the conservation of the town’s local environment. In the most recent episode of her podcast, Tanley discusses a proposed development plan for the Hapsey Wetlands by Nature+Adventure Group, who run commercial excursions into unspoilt areas. The following text is a transcript of this episode, with images provided by Tanley. Hello and welcome to Hapsey Happenings. I’m your host, Janelle. I’m sure all of you listening have heard about the recent development plan that has been proposed for the Hapsey Wetlands, one of the very last protected sanctuaries in this local region. Nature+Adventure Group’s plan includes bike paths, boat ramps, kiosks and cafes. The local newspaper is being flooded with letters and emails from people for and against the plan, and my socials have also been overflowing. Let’s consider our wetlands. They are quite a distance from the town, so they’re mainly visited by nature lovers – people who don’t mind a walk from where they can leave their car. The paths are narrow and unpaved. Walkers have to be careful but this means the native flora is taken care of. The rejection of a Melbourne CBD injecting room feels like a rejection of me Anonymous The government seems to want teams to go scoop up drug users and put them in treatment – whether they’re ready for it or not Fri 26 Apr 2024 I’ve used heroin off and on for decades now. I still do. Throughout my 20s, I had used drugs in ways that were often destructive to me. Back then, I worked 40 hours a week in a paid job a lot of the time, but at other times I was homeless, seemingly always in withdrawal, and desperate for dollars or drugs. Looking back, I was incredibly destructive. I ripped off shops, crashed cars and got in trouble with the cops. I had hepatitis C (of which I have been cured), have overdosed multiple times and developed infections from injecting – often in an alleyway or an abandoned house or a public toilet or a train. While people have issues with alcohol and other acceptable drugs that make their lives difficult, they don’t go to jail just for buying or consuming their drugs. That fact – and the drive to avoid the police, along with the awful reality of opioid withdrawal – has ensured my decision-making is sometimes focused on immediate relief or safety, rather than long-term health choices. That is why harm reduction and injecting rooms – such as the one in North Richmond – are so crucial for keeping people alive and healthy. When I heard the governme

Sample Essay I In their article for The Guardian Australia on the 26th of April 2024, titled ‘I started using heroin decades ago. The rejection of the Melbourne CBD injecting room feels like a rejection of me’, an anonymous author with a history of drug use describes how the decision not to open a second safe injecting room in Melbourne is an abandonment of drug users across the state. To an audience of mainly residents of Melbourne, but also to the wider Australian public, the author uses an upset and exasperated tone to outline how the Victorian Government’s decision to scrap plans for an injecting room in Melbourne’s CBD is essentially letting down those who use drugs and feel that safe injecting rooms are a critical part of the solution. The author initially argues why drug users can be so hesitant to ask and accept help in regard to their drug use. Through the use of anecdotal evidence, they discuss some of the experiences they have been through while using drugs. By saying ’Looking back, I was incredibly destructive”, the author highlights to the audience how dire a situation can get for those using drugs and do not have the necessary support networks around them. As such, the audience get an insight into the mindset of a drug user and start to see things slightly more from their point of view. Thus, the audience are more inclined to develop a sense of empathy for drug users, as they can somewhat see what drug users go through without any help. Additionally, the author
