Exam Format The VCE English exam is a three-hour exam, plus 15 minutes of reading time. It has three sections, and you are required to complete all three sections. Each section is worth the same number of marks, so you should spend the same amount of time on each.
Exam Specifications

Overall Conditions The examination will be sat at a time and date to be set annually by the Victorian Curriculum and Assessment Authority (VCAA). VCAA examination rules will apply. There will be 15 minutes of reading time and 3 hours of writing time. The examination will be assessed by a panel appointed by the VCAA. The examination will contribute 50 per cent to the study score.

Format The Task Book will be sixteen pages in length. The front page contains the general cover notes and instructions, including rules about what you can and can’t take into the exam. An English and/or bilingual printed dictionary is permitted. Mobile Phones and/pr any unauthrosied electronic devices are not permitted.
Strategies for Addressing the Requirements

Section A In Section A you will write an essay that responds to one of the two given topics on a text you have studied. You can choose to write on either your Unit 1 text or your Unit 4 text. In your answer book you will need to identify the text on which you are writing and the topic (i or ii) you are responding to by shading the relevant box. Spend a few minutes unpacking the topic you feel most comfortable responding to. Look carefully at each term in the topic and make sure you understand its meaning and significance. Use the strategies for identifying topic words, direction words and limiting words outlined on pages 48–50 in English Year 12. Spend about 5 minutes writing a brief plan that includes your contention, three or four topic sentences and one or two pieces of textual evidence for each topic sentence. The following strategies will help you to address the Section A requirements and criteria. Address the topic in full. Strong responses take account of every word in the topic, including the limiting words, rather than writing about one key term in a general way. Use your dictionary. Make sure you understand the meanings of all the words in the topic. Your dictionary will also give you some synonyms for key terms in the topic, and using these will give your writing more variety and precision. Consider a range of textual features. Don’t limit your reasons and evidence to just what the characters say and do. While authors do use characters to explore ideas, they also u

Section B In Section B you will write a text that responds to a given title and at least one piece of stimulus material. In the answer book, you will need to shade the box of your chosen Framework, and the box (or boxes) corresponding to the stimulus (or stimuli) you have responded to. The following strategies will help you to address the Section B criteria and weave the stimuli and title into your creative piece. Ensure your writing is clearly relevant to your chosen Framework. This involves integrating concepts and perspectives that you’ve explored during the year. When you are revising in preparation for the exam, refer to your notes and written pieces to stay aligned with the core ideas of the Framework. Integrate the title and at least one stimulus into your writing. You must use at least one of the stimuli. The stimulus should not just be referenced superficially; it should be woven into the fabric of your piece. This could mean, for example, using a text stimulus as the basis for a central idea; using a text stimulus as a direct quote at a key point in your text; or drawing inspiration from a visual stimulus to set a scene. Similarly, the title provided for your Framework should provide an idea, a character arc, a central image or another key element of your writing. Give your piece cohesion and structure. Your piece should have a clear beginning, middle and end, regardless of whether it’s a narrative, an argument or a reflective piece. Each part should seamlessly tran

Section C In Section C you will analyse a text in which the writer seeks to persuade a particular audience to agree with their point of view on an issue. The text will be primarily written but will also contain one or more visual elements. This task has been part of the VCE English exam for many years and as part of your preparation you may use past exam papers as sources of practice material. In some cases you will find that the task material consists of more than one written piece, but the exam specifications for 2024–2027 indicate that there will only be a single ‘unseen persuasive text’ in the English exams during this period. The following strategies will help you to address the Section C requirements and criteria. Understand the contention. What, essentially, is the writer saying? An accurate understanding of the writer’s central argument underpins your analysis. Clearly state the writer’s contention in your introduction. Read the background information carefully. This is where you will find out the intended audience and the context for the text. Your analysis will be strengthened by a consideration of how the writer’s choices of argument and language are shaped by their particular audience and context (see the final bullet point below). Examine the sequencing of the argument. Writers will arrange their material and their points of argument in a particular order so as to engage their intended audience and influence their thoughts and feelings. Look at the overall shape

Practice Exams This section of the book contains practice exams. These are not the previous VCAA exams which one may find on the official website. These exams have been specially made in order to assist you with better preparation for the exam.

t: General Checklist for Section A Response
b: General Checklist for Section A Response

Textual Understanding and Interpretation Have I demonstrated a clear and accurate understanding of the text? Have I explored the ideas, concerns, and values present in the text? Have I considered the structure and language used in the text to convey meaning? Have I shown awareness of the complexities or subtleties in the text’s ideas?

Response to the Topic Have I clearly addressed the essay topic throughout my response? Have I interpreted the implications of the topic (not just the literal meaning)? Have I developed a relevant and thoughtful argument or discussion? Have I used an appropriate strategy to explore the topic (e.g., thematic, character-based, structural)?
Use of Evidence Have I supported my ideas with specific and relevant evidence from the text? Have I explained how my evidence supports my argument? Have I used quotations effectively and accurately?

Organisation and Coherence Is my essay well-structured, with a clear introduction, body, and conclusion? Are my ideas logically sequenced and connected? Have I written clear topic sentences and developed each paragraph?
Language Use Is my language accurate, appropriate, and mostly free of errors? Have I used precise and expressive vocabulary to convey meaning? Is my writing fluent and confident in tone?

t: General Checklist for Section B Response
b: General Checklist for Section B Response

Engagement with Ideas and Stimulus Have I considered the ideas raised by the title and stimulus in a thoughtful and meaningful way? Have I connected these ideas to a clear and relevant Framework of Ideas? Have I explored the implications or complexities of the ideas, rather than treating them superficially?

Purpose and Voice Have I clearly established the purpose(s) of my text? Have I maintained an appropriate and consistent voice throughout the piece? Is the form of the piece suited to the intended audience and purpose (e.g., speech, monologue, letter)?

Control of Language Have I used language that is clear, expressive, and appropriate for the form? Have I used a rich and precise vocabulary to strengthen my writing? Have I varied sentence structure and language features to create impact and cohesion?

Control of Text Structure Is my writing well-organised and easy to follow? Have I structured the text purposefully, with a clear beginning, middle, and end? Have I used paragraphs and transitions effectively to guide the reader?

t: General Checklist for Section C Response
b: General Checklist for Section C Response

Understanding of Argument and Contention Have I accurately identified the contention of the text? Have I understood how the argument(s) are structured and developed across the text? Have I recognised the overall point of view being expressed by the author/speaker?

t: Analysis of Persuasive Language and Visuals
b: Analysis of Persuasive Language and Visuals Have I analysed how language features (written or spoken) are used to persuade the intended audience? Have I explained how visuals (if any) support or enhance the written or spoken argument? Have I discussed how these elements work together to construct the overall persuasive strategy?

Expression and Language Use Have I used language that is clear, precise, and appropriate to an analytical task? Have I written fluently and used analytical vocabulary confidently? Have I maintained a consistent and formal tone suitable for academic analysis?

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: What Worked Students clearly understood the structure and expectations of the exam. Many responses demonstrated well-developed writing and analytical skills.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: What Needs Improvement Some students forgot to clearly label their chosen text (Section A) or Framework of Ideas (Section B). Some responses misinterpreted the prompt or overlooked key words in the question.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: Practical Tips Label your work clearly. Always begin with: Section A: ’Text: Macbeth | Prompt: ‘Ambition leads to downfall. Do you agree?’” Section B: ’Title: Resist and Persist | Framework: Protest” Build your vocabulary. Familiarise yourself with words like inescapable, perceptions, central concern, implication, etc. Select the best-fit prompt. Choose the one that allows you to demonstrate your strongest understanding, not just the one that sounds easiest.

t: Section A: Analytical Response to a Text
p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: Section A: Analytical Response to a Text Your task: Write a structured essay in response to one of two prompts for your studied text.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: What Worked High-scoring responses interpreted the topic with nuance and clarity. Students demonstrated a strong understanding of authorial intent. Responses were well structured and supported with relevant textual evidence.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: What Needs Improvement Some students only responded to part of the question (e.g. discussing ’youth” but not ’infatuation with youth”). Others summarised the plot or characters instead of analysing the text’s ideas and values.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: Practical Tips ’ Tip: Write practice introductions where you break down the prompt. Ask: What is the main idea or concept? Are there two ideas in relationship? What are the implications or assumptions?

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: Section B - Creating a Text (Framework of Ideas) Your task: Create an original piece of writing that responds to a title and stimulus, grounded in one of the four Frameworks of Ideas.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: What Worked High-scoring pieces treated the title and stimulus as central, not secondary. Strong voice, clear purpose, and tailored language made the writing stand out. Responses showed thoughtful exploration of ideas relevant to the Framework.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: What Needs Improvement Some students ignored or misunderstood the Framework (e.g. writing about sports under the Protest framework). Others forced pre-prepared stories to fit the title. Lack of planning resulted in unstructured or unfocused pieces.

p: Key Takeaways from the 2024 Assessment Report
b: Final Advice The 2024 exam rewarded students who thought deeply, wrote purposefully, and tailored their writing to the demands of the task. Whether you’re analysing a text, creating your own piece, or evaluating argument and language, success lies in showing insight, clarity, and control. Strong writing does not come from memorisation—it comes from understanding. Prepare thoughtfully, practise deliberately, and revise reflectively.

t: Key Takeaways from the 2025 Assessment Report
p: Key Takeaways from the 2025 Assessment Report
b: Part 12 Key Takeaways from the 2025 Assessment Report What the VCAA assessors reported from the 2025 English examination, broken down by section — the marking approach, the strategies that earned top marks, the pitfalls that cost them, and a priority checklist distilled for the 2026 cohort. In this part 1 General Exam Advice 2 Section A: Analytical Response to a Text 3 Section B: Creating a Text 4 Section C: Analysis of Argument and Language 5 Priority Checklist for 2026

Part 12 General Exam Advice The 2025 examination produced near-identical averages across all three sections — 5.4, 5.4 and 5.5 out of 10 — with very few students at either extreme. The middle is crowded; the marks that separate a 5 from an 8 come from the specific skills assessors described, not from writing more. Section Average /10 What it was A 5.4 Analytical response to a text B 5.4 Creating a text (Frameworks of Ideas) C 5.5 Analysis of argument and language How assessors marked Holistic, criteria-based, benchmarked. Every section was assessed against the published criteria and expected qualities (EQs), using benchmark scripts to set standards. The three EQs are interconnected: the quality of your ideas (EQ1) sets the level of challenge for structuring and substantiating them (EQ2), and language (EQ3) is judged by how well it communicates those ideas. Everything is applied in relation to the topic. Sound ideas, apt quotations or fluent expression that do not serve the set task cannot score highly. This principle recurs through every section of the report. First-draft conditions were respected. Assessors explicitly acknowledged that exam writing is timed first-draft work; fluency was not equated with perfection. Administrative habits that mattered Shade the box for your chosen text and topic in Section A, and for your Framework and stimulus in Section B — and write the Section B title at the start of your response. Choose the topic that best lets you demonstrate your know

t: Section A: Analytical Response to a Text
p: Section A: Analytical Response to a Text
b: Part 12 Section A: Analytical Response to a Text The 2025 topics were deliberately narrow — specified ideas and values rather than broad prompts — and this shaped how responses needed to be structured, sequenced and evidenced. Students with topic-interpretation strategies handled both familiar and unfamiliar phrasings well. Topic types Propositional topics offered a proposition with a command term: Discuss , Do you agree? or To what extent do you agree? You may challenge the proposition where warranted, but never the topic's conceptual focus itself — a topic linking love and destruction requires discussing both, though "inseparable" can be contested. Quotation topics paired a quotation with a proposition. There was no obligatory way to use the quotation, but how you used it revealed your understanding of what it conveys in context. The verbs The verb in a topic carries the author's purpose, and flattening every verb into a generic "presents" was unwise. Assessors also warned that verbs are often nominalised or implied, and new ones appear each year. Used in 2025 New in 2025 celebrates, challenges, condemns, demonstrates, displays, explores, highlights, mocks, shows, suggests is (a cry for), depicts, glorifies, warns Implications: where the top marks lived There was a direct correlation between recognising a topic's implications and higher scores. Watch for: absolute terms ("there are no right choices"), connotation (" easy to overlook "), what a proposition is silent about, c

Part 12 Section B: Creating a Text Nearly half the state wrote on protest, yet all four Frameworks averaged within 0.4 marks of each other — choose the Framework that suits your ideas, not the popular one. Framework % of students Average score /20 Writing about country 10% 11.2 Writing about protest 47% 10.9 Writing about personal journeys 25% 10.9 Writing about play 17% 10.8 The rules of the task The title is fixed. You cannot change or modify it; assessors ignored any additions. Choosing a title selects its Framework — no mixing and matching titles, Frameworks or stimulus sets. If your shaded box and your title disagreed, assessors followed the title. Write the title at the start and shade both the Framework and stimulus boxes. Stimulus ideas must come from your selected Framework's stimulus material, though how you use them is open. Mentor texts don't need referencing , and there is no preferred form, style, purpose or audience — responses could explain, express, reflect or argue, and could adapt or subvert any prose form. What lifted responses Using the title as the focus while exploring Framework ideas purposefully through a cohesive text. Making deliberate structural and linguistic choices that amplify ideas — the annotated high scorers moved economically between place and time, used imagery to imply location, and chose words that carried complex emotion. Establishing a voice that connects strategically with the reader — one exemplar built "a thoughtful, confiding refle

t: Section C: Analysis of Argument and Language
p: Section C: Analysis of Argument and Language
b: Part 12 Section C: Analysis of Argument and Language The 2025 text was a letter to a community newsletter from a "local resident" proposing that a regional town replace its annual fireworks with an alternative celebration. Students found it highly accessible — which made the discriminators sharper. Background information is not decoration The publication details and the author's "local resident" persona had to be used: inferring the regional audience and its values, the author's altruistic credibility, and the unhurried "evolution, not urgency" framing enabled the most focused analyses. Read the background inferentially before writing — the analysis flows from it. The argument structure most students saw Establishing a shared value: love of the celebration in its beautiful setting. Raising previously unconsidered problems — environment, frightened pets (with a photo), noise-sensitive residents, cost — aligned with community values. Rebutting reasons to keep the status quo. Presenting the alternative as a "win-win" (with an inspiring second visual). What separated skill levels Identification is not analysis. Most students could list arguments and label techniques; far fewer could explain how the sequencing of ideas served the purpose, or what a device actually did to the intended audience. Interplay was the discriminator. The strongest responses explained how argument, written language and visuals worked together — nearly everyone noticed the frightened dog photo, but skill sh

Part 12 Priority Checklist for 2026 The habits below fall straight out of the 2025 report — each one addresses something assessors explicitly rewarded or penalised. Before the exam Build a topic-interpretation routine: identify the conceptual focus, the command term, the verb, and at least two implications before planning. Rehearse unfamiliar-verb strategy — and pack a dictionary. Practise writing to a fixed title in Section B without bending a pre-prepared piece to fit. For Section C practice, always start from the background information and write down audience, purpose and author positioning before analysing. In the exam Shade every box: text and topic (Section A); Framework and stimulus (Section B) — and write the Section B title first. Section A: open and close each paragraph with sentences that link your ideas to the topic; use the conclusion to resolve the question. Section B: keep the title in view as you write; make every structural and word choice serve your purpose and reader. Section C: select the few elements most central to the persuasion and explain their interplay — argument, language and visuals together. Everywhere: sophisticated language only where it communicates a sophisticated idea. Summarised from the VCAA 2025 English examination report. Read alongside the 2024 takeaways and the assessment criteria .
Assess your current study habits

Identifying strengths and weaknesses Take a few minutes to jot down what’s working well in your current study routine. What areas could use some improvement? Consider factors like time management, focus, and understanding of different subjects.

Setting realistic goals Based on your assessment, you could start by setting 2-3 specific, achievable goals for improving your study habits. Make these goals SMART: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound. Write them down and place them somewhere visible as a daily reminder.

Create a personalised study schedule Now that you know where you stand, it’s time to map out a path to exam success. Think of your study schedule as a roadmap to help you navigate your way through VCE studies.

Time management tips List all your subjects and the topics you need to cover for each. Assess how much time you have until your exams. Consider prioritising subjects based on difficulty and your current performance. Using a study schedule template Remember: Be realistic with your schedule. If you’re not a morning person, a sunrise study session may not suit you. Try to work with your natural rhythms, and not against them.

t: Find a study schedule template that works for you – digital or physical, whichever works best.
b: Find a study schedule template that works for you – digital or physical, whichever works best. Block out fixed commitments like school, extracurriculars, and family time. Allocate study blocks for each subject, ensuring a mix of subjects each day. Don’t forget to schedule breaks and ’me time’ to avoid feelings of burnout.
Organise your study materials A cluttered space can contribute to a cluttered mind. Here are some strategies to effectively organise your study materials.

Gathering resources Collect all your textbooks, notes, and past papers for each subject. Organise digital resources into clearly labelled folders on your computer. Consider creating a ’study toolkit’ with essentials like highlighters, sticky notes, and index cards.

Creating a study-friendly environment Find a quiet, well-lit space where you can focus without distractions. Ensure you have a comfortable chair and desk at the right height. Keep your study area clean and organised – a tidy space can do wonders for your concentration.

t: Implement active recall and spaced repetition
b: Implement active recall and spaced repetition Below are some tried and true study techniques you might like to explore.

The power of self-testing After studying a topic, close your books and try to recall as much as you can. Create flashcards for key concepts and test yourself regularly. Explain concepts out loud as if you’re teaching someone else.
Spacing out your study sessions Avoid cramming by spreading out your study sessions over time. Review material at increasing intervals: e.g. 1 day later, 3 days later, 1 week later, etc.

t: Utilise practice exams and past papers
b: Utilise practice exams and past papers Another way to effectively prepare for exams is by practising past exams. Here are some techniques you might find helpful.

Simulating exam conditions Find past papers or practice exams for your subjects. Set up a quiet space and time yourself as you would in a real exam. Avoid using your notes or textbooks during these practice sessions.

Analysing your performance Mark your practice exams using the relevant marking schemes, if available. Identify areas where you lost marks and focus on improving these in your study sessions. Keep a log of common mistakes to avoid repeating them in the real exam.
Develop a healthy study-life balance It’s important to look after yourself and practice self-care during this time. Find what works best for you, or try our ideas below.

Incorporating breaks and relaxation Use techniques like the Pomodoro method: 25 minutes of focused study followed by a 5-minute break. Schedule longer breaks for activities you enjoy – maybe a quick game of basketball or a chat with friends. Practice mindfulness or meditation to help manage stress and improve focus.

Maintaining physical and mental well-being Aim for 7-9 hours of sleep per night – your brain consolidates memories and learning during sleep. Incorporate regular exercise to improve your mood and reduce stress. Eat a balanced diet with brain-boosting foods like blueberries, nuts, and fatty fish.
Collaborate with study groups Two heads are better than one, and a study group can be a powerhouse of knowledge, inspiration and motivation.

The benefits of peer learning Explaining concepts to others can help solidify your own understanding. Gain new perspectives and insights from your peers. Stay motivated and accountable with group study sessions.
Organising effective study sessions Set clear objectives for each study session. Use tools like shared Google Docs or virtual whiteboards for collaborative note-taking.
Seek help when needed Remember, asking for help isn’t a sign of weakness.
Utilising teacher support Don’t hesitate to approach your teachers with specific questions. Attend any extra help sessions offered by your school or community. Ask for feedback on your work.

Exploring additional resources Explore online tutorials or video explanations for challenging topics. Consider getting a tutor for subjects where you’re really struggling. Join online forums or study groups for your specific subjects.
Fine-tune your exam day strategy When your exams are approaching – make sure you’re feeling prepared.
Pre-exam preparation Get a good night’s sleep before the exam. Eat a nutritious breakfast to fuel your brain. Arrive at the exam venue early to avoid last-minute stress.

During the exam tactics Read through the entire paper before starting to answer questions. Manage your time wisely – allocate minutes to each section based on mark values. If you get stuck on a question, move on and come back to it later.

Study Tools Glossary of Techniques Every film and persuasive technique from the guide in one alphabetical reference. Adjective Persuasive technique Describing words usually connected to a noun (naming word). e.g. The bright red house (bright and red = adjectives; house = noun) — Adds emphasis. Aerial shot Film technique A shot with the camera positioned directly above a subject. Alliteration Persuasive technique Repeating and playing upon the same letter. e.g. A back-breaking job... — Adds emphasis and reinforces meaning, especially where an idea is repeated. Often used to create a highly emotive image. Anecdote Persuasive technique A short account or story of an entertaining or interesting incident. e.g. In my experience... — Usually makes reader sympathetic and receptive to the point; can set up a character, then position reader to accept or reject that individual character. Associations Persuasive technique Words that create meanings beyond the literal meaning of the word. e.g. attractive, alluring, pleasing, charming — Suggests or influences reader response in certain ways, often subconsciously. Implied meanings need to be identified. Attacks/praise Persuasive technique The writer attacks or praises an opponent or idea. This is really an assertion of the writer’s viewpoint. e.g. denigrating or humiliating the person, discrediting opponents as unreliable, dishonest, suggesting unsatisfactory associations — Often effective in placing the reader in a position of agreement wi
